pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_10
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 10 (serials 448 onward), a 184-page compilation of correspondence and internal memoranda spanning roughly 1966 through 1974 in which the Bureau handled UFO inquiries from private citizens. Letters signed by Director J. Edgar Hoover and later Acting/Director Clarence M. Kelley repeatedly tell correspondents (Mrs. Levi J. Dow of Goffstown NH, Mary Beth Martine, Ron Fraide of La Habra CA, the Ajax Ontario UFO Astronomer Club, Larry W. Bryant, J. A. Hennessey of London, and others) that investigation of unidentified flying objects falls outside FBI jurisdiction. The file also embeds AFSCA Flying Saucers International magazine clippings, a 1972 anonymous letter threatening to leak Pentagon UFO documents to the New York Times, a 1971 ACSI Army intelligence routing slip from LTC Alfred G. Keggins, and a March 1973 San Antonio FBI LHM by SA Richard T. Jesinger about Sgt. Paul Stigliano's report on a man calling himself Air Force Captain Dave T. Ozanne who solicited Waco Tribune UFO information while carrying a Top Secret folder.
pdfs.officialBlurb: The FBI's 62-HQ-83894 case file includes investigative records, eyewitness testimonies, and public reports concerning Unidentified Flying Objects and flying discs documented between June 1947 and July 1968. The records include high-profile incident accounts, photographic evidence from sites like Oak Ridge, TN, and technical proposals regarding potential propulsion systems. Additional topics include convention programs, researcher accounts, and extensive media coverage from the period.  This file is partially posted on FBI vault with more redactions and some pages missing.  Included here is the complete case file with several newly declassified pages and only minor redactions.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_2
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 2 (serials 53-100), a 194-page run of 1947 cables, SAC letters, teletypes and Office Memoranda routing the earliest 'flying disc' reports to Director Hoover and Assistant Director D. M. Ladd. SAC San Francisco forwards Fourth Air Force A-2 reports from Lt. Col. Donald Springer and Maj. William R. Graham at Hamilton Field; SAC Newark documents an August 3, 1947 sighting at Hackensack NJ by Charles Casella Jr., Pvt. William Truex of Fort Dix and Joyce McFarland; SAC Detroit teletypes the Raymond Edward and Laura Lane 'fused sand' incident at Dow Chemical, Midland MI; and SAC Chicago is directed to interview Richard F. Shaver of Lily Lake IL after an anonymous July 5 telegram to AAF HQ tied him to flying-disc origins, alongside Maj. John D. Schindler Jr.'s July 7 report on saucers tracked over southern Wisconsin.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_3
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 3 (Serials 101-130), is a 190-page compilation of declassified records from 1947 covering the earliest 'flying disc' reports. The bundle includes Office Memorandums between SACs (San Francisco, Seattle, Butte) and FBI Director, plus correspondence and Summary of Information forms from Lt. Col. Donald L. Springer (A-2, Fourth Air Force, Hamilton Field), Major Jack C. White, and other Army Air Forces intelligence officers. Items document witness statements such as F.M. Johnson of Portland, Birmingham photograph reports, the Maury Island/Tacoma Fred Crisman and Harold Dahl affair (including Kenneth Arnold's interview and the death of Captain Davidson in the B-25 1316 crash), and sightings over Guam. The OCR-extracted text retains classification stamps ('CONFIDENTIAL,' 'RESTRICTED'), with serial numbers like 62-83894-101 through 130 visible alongside indexing marks.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_4
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 4 (Serials 131-185), containing 1947-1948 SAC field reports and Director Hoover correspondence on flying disc sightings. Includes a detailed Portland SAC report on the September 11, 1947 sightings by Chief of Police Leon Jenkins and Officers Adair, Caldwell, Raney, Shaylor and Cowling; an Anchorage SAC report on a July 1947 Bethel, Alaska airborne sighting by pilots Peck and Vince Daly in a DC-3; an Albany SAC report closing out informant A. Courtney Parker of South Royalton, Vermont; and Hoover acknowledgment letters to civilian correspondents including Mrs. McCaul, Mrs. Durand, Joseph Omiline, and a Port Allegany report quoting Red Cross director Joe Lippincott's claim that saucers originated in Spain.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_5
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 5 (Serials 186-245), 209 pages of correspondence from mid-1949 routed through Director Hoover, D.M. Ladd, Tolson and other Bureau officials. Centers on the Peter Camerlon Jones case: a letter Jones wrote to columnist Walter Winchell describing a 1947 mountain encounter near Los Angeles with a silver top-shaped object, prompting urgent teletypes to SAC Los Angeles R.B. Hood for background and interview, Ernest Cuneo of New York acting as Winchell's intermediary, and a July 26, 1949 SAC San Antonio memo on a Young Men's Business Club flying-saucer convention in Alexandria, Louisiana. Routine Bureau memoranda on flying disk and saucer reports.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_6
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 6 (Serials 246-301), a 271-page compilation of inter-office memoranda from 1950 covering aerial phenomena investigations. Includes A.H. Belmont's August 1950 summary memo to D.M. Ladd on green fireballs, discs and meteors near sensitive New Mexico installations, Dr. Lincoln La Paz's analysis through the University of New Mexico's Institute of Meteoritics, the Air Force/Land-Air Inc. Project Twinkle observation effort at Vaughn, NM, a September 1950 letter from FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover to USAF Director of Special Investigations forwarding a Toronto correspondent's saucer letter, and a Major General C.P. Cabell USAF directive AFOIO-CC-1 dated 8 September 1950 standardizing reporting of unconventional aircraft to Air Materiel Command. Also documents a Philadelphia Police flying-disc residue sighting on 9/26/1950.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_7
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 7 (Serials 302-343), comprising correspondence from August through September 1952 between Director J. Edgar Hoover, FBI field offices, and the Air Force Office of Special Investigations regarding flying saucer reports. Items include a Savannah teletype on a blue saucer-shaped light over the AEC's Savannah River Plant, Hoover form-letter replies forwarding civilian sightings (Edmond Kane, Mrs. Tygrett, Mrs. Haufe, William Hoffmeyer, John Lang, Edmond Elwell) to the Air Force, a German-language anonymous letter translated by the Cryptanalysis-Translation Section, lab examination of scoutmaster D.S. Desvergers's burned cap from West Palm Beach, and Branigan-to-Belmont memos reissuing SAC Letter #38 instructions to refer disc complaints to OSI.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_9
pdfs.blurb: FBI headquarters file Section 9 (serials 385-447) compiling internal Bureau correspondence on flying-disc reports from late 1957 through the early 1960s, including Office Memoranda from SACs in Dallas, St. Louis, and Kansas City, a Roach-to-Belmont memo on the post-Sputnik wave of UFO sightings, and outbound letters signed by Director J. Edgar Hoover to the Air Force Director of Special Investigations referring civilian inquiries. Materials cover Robert T. Stone's Inter-Continental Aerial Research Foundation, Donald E. Keyhoe's NICAP queries, William A. Rhodes' 1947 Phoenix saucer photographs, the Aerial Phenomena Research Group, and a Sanders memo summarizing standing Bureau policy that all UFO reports be referred to the Air Force without FBI investigation.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_130
pdfs.blurb: Serial 130 of FBI file 62-HQ-83894, declassified under NND 90986, gathers Air Defense Command and Fourth Air Force intelligence summaries together with FBI office memoranda from 1947. Featured items include 15-18 September 1947 Headquarters Air Defense Command (Mitchel Field, NY) Summary of Information interviews with Pan American Airways Captain Alpheus O. Powell and navigator Walter I. White concerning a 4 August 1947 sighting of an orange cylindrical object near Boston during a Gander-La Guardia flight, and an 8 July 1947 Birmingham AAF report by Major Jack C. White transmitting a photograph by Age-Herald copy reader Robert Crossland of discs over Birmingham, Alabama. Additional pages reproduce newspaper clippings, witness statements from Twin Falls and Filer (Idaho) including County Commissioner Hawkins, and a Williams Field/Brooks Field interview with Lt. Eric B. Armstrong about objects near Lake Mead. Pages bear 'CONFIDENTIAL' and 'RESTRICTED' stamps and routing marks.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_164
pdfs.blurb: Serial 164 of FBI file 62-HQ-83894 reproduces 'Air Intelligence Requirements Memorandum Number 4 - Unconventional Aircraft,' issued 15 February 1949 by the Directorate of Intelligence, Headquarters United States Air Force, Washington, signed by Major General C.P. Cabell, Director of Intelligence. The memo rescinds Department of the Army Collection Memorandum Number 7 (21 January 1948) and CSGID 425.1 (25 March 1948), and prescribes reporting channels through the Commanding General, Air Materiel Command at Wright-Patterson AFB (MCIAXO-3) and the Director of Intelligence, USAF. Part II enumerates required data fields for sightings, including object color, shape, propulsion, exhaust, radar returns, observer reliability checks (including FBI records), Geiger-counter radiation checks, and soil sampling at landing sites. Distribution lists CIA, State Department, Naval Intelligence, Coast Guard, and the FBI Director; the 137-page file repeats the memorandum and is marked 'RESTRICTED.'

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_220
pdfs.blurb: FBI HQ Serial 220 (62-HQ-83894-EBF 220), a 15-page enclosure-behind-file consisting of a March 19, 1950 letter and inventor's manuscript from Miguel Angel Garcia Macias of Veracruz, Mexico, addressed to the President of the Commission of Scientific Investigation of the United States of North America in New York. Garcia Macias, identifying himself as an Ideographic Inventor, describes his theory of stratospheric aerostats (flying saucers) using atomic propulsion and global-conic stability, complains his prior inventions (Gote-Graduns dropper, automatic shovels) were patented by others, and encloses a Spanish-language original, hand-drawn diagrams of propellers and stability cones, and a March 16, 1950 Mexican newspaper clipping about photographs of a flying saucer over Durango by student German Horacio Robles Jr., translated by Mrs. Sophia Saliba.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_403
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters cover sheet and dust-jacket reproduction filed as Serial 403 of file 62-83894, consisting of the front, back and inside flap copy from Gray Barker's 1956 University Books volume 'They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers'. The jacket text promotes Barker, a Clarksburg, West Virginia film-booking businessman and publisher of The Saucerian, and his account of saucer researchers allegedly silenced by 'three men in dark suits' after a 1952 West Virginia landing. A publisher's letter from H.G. Rhawn of the Clarksburg News is referenced as endorsement. Fully unredacted under FBI Automatic Declassification.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_438
pdfs.blurb: FBI Albuquerque office report dated May 8, 1964 by Special Agent D. Arthur Byrnes, Jr. documenting the Lonnie Zamora UFO incident at Socorro, New Mexico on April 24, 1964. Byrnes describes arriving on scene with New Mexico State Police Sergeant M. S. Chavez, Officer Ted Jordan, and Undersheriff Jim Luckie, observing four rectangular ground indentations, three burned grass clumps, and three small circular marks; the report attaches Officer Zamora's first-person narrative of chasing a speeder, hearing a roar, seeing a shiny egg-shaped object on landing legs with two white-coveralled figures, and watching it ascend with blue-orange flame, plus a hand sketch of the red insignia. The serial includes multiple carbon copies of the same memorandum and a diagram of the burn area and footprints.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_449
pdfs.blurb: FBI airtel from the SAC, Los Angeles to the Director dated October 3, 1966 (file 100-24442) forwarding Issue No. 24 (July 1966) of Flying Saucers International, the official journal of the Amalgamated Flying Saucer Clubs of America headquartered at 2004 North Hoover Street, Los Angeles. The Philadelphia Division had received the magazine from Mr. Jarvis H. Cooper of the IRS, who flagged a front-page article that allegedly expounded the Communist Party line. The article, attributed to 'Master Kalen-Li Retan' of the planet Korendor and relayed via short-wave by Bob Renaud, denounces the U.S. Military-Industrial complex and the Vietnam War; Los Angeles indicates no investigation contemplated. Lightly redacted under standard FBI Automatic Declassification.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_SUB_A
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file (62-83894 Sub A) compiling newspaper clippings and wire-service stories from the late 1940s through the mid-1960s about flying saucer sightings and UFO controversies, routed via the Bureau's clipping desk to officials including Tolson, Belmont, Mohr, Rosen, and Miss Gandy. Stories cover the 1959 American Airlines DC-6 sighting by Capt. Peter Killian over Pennsylvania, Carl Jung's 1958 statements at Alamogordo, the 1957 Reinhold Schmidt Kearney, Nebraska contactee episode, the 1953 Quantico Marines incident, Capt. Ulf Christiernsson's Swedish saucer report, and disputes between Maj. Donald Keyhoe and the Air Force over Project Blue Book findings. Pages bear FBI routing stamps and the case-file serial 62-83894-A; the file is fully unredacted under FBI's automatic declassification.

pdfs.title: 18_100754_ General 1946-7_Vol_2
pdfs.blurb: This file contains memorandums and correspondence related to flying disc/saucer sightings and that those are a matter of concern for the Air Materiel Command.

pdfs.title: 18_6369445_General_1948_Vol_1
pdfs.blurb: USAF Directorate of Intelligence routing folder (Project SIGN / Flying Discs, 1948 General Vol. 1), 28 pages of indorsements and correspondence forwarded from HQ USAF (AFOIR-CO, Lt. Col. Garrett) to Air Materiel Command at Wright-Patterson AFB. Contains an Eleventh Air Force report of an 8 May 1948 phosphorescent disc sighting at Hobson, Ohio (witnesses Ben Lupe, Earl Roush, Patrolman C.K. Hite of the New York Central System), a 24 September 1947 AMC letter from Col. H.M. McCoy on the Loedding LD-2 disc and Horten brothers tailless aircraft (RAE Tech Note AERO 1703), Fourth Air Force reports on March 1948 Bakersfield, California sightings, and a February 1948 G-2/AMC exchange (Brig. Gen. Cabell, Maj. Gen. McDonald, Maj. Gen. Anderson) rejecting continuous fighter alert as infeasible.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This file contains memorandums, correspondence, and forms related to the reporting of information on flying discs and investigations into sightings.

pdfs.title: 255_413270_UFO's_and_Defense_What_Should_we_Prepare_For
pdfs.blurb: This file contains an independent report on UFOs written by the French association COMETA (previously published in the French magazine VDS in 1999), which details the results of a study by the Institute of Higher Studies for National Defence. The file also includes a letter from Carol Rosin in which she notes that she was spokesperson for von Braun during the last years of his life.

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D3, Gemini 7 Transcript, 1965
pdfs.blurb: NASA air-to-ground voice transcript (Tape T-00763 R1b) capturing the Public Affairs Office release commentary of the Gemini 7 / Gemini 6 rendezvous flight on 5 December 1965. Astronaut Frank Borman calls out a bogey at ten o'clock high to Houston, with crewmate Jim Lovell adding that the object is at his two o'clock position, slowly tumbling, alongside hundreds of small particles and the booster, three to four miles away from the spacecraft. The PAO frames the sighting as a third unidentified object beyond the booster and particles, occurring at 4 hours 24 minutes mission elapsed time. Includes both a typewritten transcript and a faintly legible handwritten copy.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Gemini 7 was the tenth crewed American spaceflight. This document is a transcript of communications between the flight crew, Astronauts James “Jim” Lovell and Frank Borman, and the Manned Flight Center (now known as Johnson Space Center) in Houston, Texas. The transcript begins with Borman’s report of a “bogey,” contemporary nomenclature for an unknown aircraft, as well as a debris field. Borman described the debris field as consisting of “very, very many […] hundreds of little particles.” He estimated the particles’ distance from the spacecraft to be four miles. Lovell described observing a “brilliant body in the sun against a black background with trillions of particles on it.”
This document also includes handwritten notes documenting the encounter, annotated with the phrase “UFO Sighting by Borman” in the top right corner.

pdfs.title: 331_120752_Numeric_Files_1944–1945_37153_German_Armament_Equipment_Documents
pdfs.blurb: Secret SHAEF Air Staff file 37153/A-2 from early 1945 documenting wartime Allied investigation into night light phenomena (foo-fighters) and unidentified cylindrical objects over the European theater. The folder contains correspondence among Air Commodore C. M. Grierson at SHAEF (Forward), Group Captain E. D. M. Hopkins of Air Ministry DDI3, A.D.I.(K) Wing Commander S. D. Felkin, and First Tactical Air Force, including Lt. Col. Leavitt Corning's XII TAC memorandum and the 415th Night Fighter Squadron's 30 January 1945 second indorsement signed by Captain F. B. Ringwald compiling sortie-report extracts of pilot encounters with red, orange and white lights over Erstein, Breisach, Sarrebourg-Strasbourg, Hagenau and other locations between 14 December 1944 and 30 January 1945. Also included is a IX TAC report and photo-attempt by the 67th Tactical Reconnaissance Group of a 12-foot aluminum cylindrical object near F-5710 on 1 March 1945, with Air Ministry suggesting Me.262s or flak rockets as the most likely explanation.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This file contains SHAEF messages and memorandums related to "night phenomena (foofighters)," flak rockets, unidentified cylindrical objects, and blinking lights. The documents include multiple references to the observations of the 415th Night Fighter Squadron.

pdfs.title: 341_110448_Records_Relating_to_the_Collection_and_Dissemination_of_Intelligence_1948-1955-TS_CONT_No.2_2-5300-2-5399
pdfs.blurb: USAF Directorate of Intelligence Top Secret Control file (TS CONT. NO. 2-5300-2-5399, 4B-854 Pentagon), seven pages of cover sheets and signal extracts from teletype TT 1524 dated 4 November 1948, addressed to General Cabell. USAFE items 10, 14 and 16 forward an OI/OB report on a high-performance unidentified jet aircraft sighted at 30,000 feet over 5155N/0355E off the Dutch coast on 5 September 1948 by three crews of the 307th Bomb Group during Operation DAGGER; a flying saucer hovering thirty minutes over Neubiberg Air Base; and Swedish Air Intelligence's conclusion, after a salvage dive recovered an uncharted lake-floor crater, that flying saucer phenomena cannot be credited to any known earthly culture and may originate elsewhere. Item USAFE 16 reports CIC source claims of Soviet purchasing of foreign radar tubes and a complete radar set.
pdfs.officialBlurb: An Air Force intelligence report from November 1948 relating to unidentified flying objects and flying saucers.

pdfs.title: 341_110677_Numerical_File,_5-2500
pdfs.blurb: Secret/NoForn USAF Air Intelligence Information Report IR-193-55 dated 30 October 1955, prepared by the U.S. Air Attache in Prague (signed T. S. Ryan) transmitting an eyewitness account of two flying-disc sightings reported by Senator Richard Russell, Lt. Col. E. U. Hathaway and consultant Ruben Efron during a 4 October 1955 rail journey between Atjaty and Adzhijabul in the Trans-Caucasus. The report describes two circular craft taking off vertically about a minute apart with rotating outer surfaces and stationary upper lights, ascending to roughly 6,000 feet before accelerating north, accompanied by Soviet trainmen who closed curtains and barred further observation. Supplementary sections cover related observations of a Soviet jet bomber at Dnieperpetrovsk airfield, radar sites near Moscow, a T-34 tank near Sochi, and the railway gauge-switching procedure at Cop, Czechoslovakia, with hand-drawn sketches.
pdfs.officialBlurb: Air Intelligence Information Report, 14 October 1955, Report of eye witness account of the ascent and flight of a unconventional aircraft in the trans-Caucasus region on the USSR.

pdfs.title: 342_HS1-416511228_319.1 Flying Discs 1949
pdfs.blurb: Department of the Air Force compilation of 1949 Flight Service Center reports (file 319.1) on unidentified flying objects, transmitted on Form HqMAAMA No. 7 to the Commanding General, Air Materiel Command, Wright-Patterson AFB. Reports cover sightings near Griffiss AFB, Rome, NY (signed by Lt. Col. Joseph L. Eil at Olmsted FSC); over Seattle and Medford, Oregon (Lt. Col. Earl J. Livesay, McChord FSC) including a 22 August 1949 disc observed by National Guard 143rd AC&W controllers; Greenville, SC (Major George G. Neffinger, Maxwell FSC, attributed to a weather balloon); Spokane (civilian pilot Bill Miller's eight dish-shaped objects); and Columbus, Ohio (CAA controllers Andees, Markwith, and Lutz). Mostly marked Restricted or Confidential.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This file primarily contains incident reports on Unidentified Flying Objects (UFOs) written in compliance with the 1948 Flight Service Regulation (FSR) 200-4. The incidents were witnessed by military sources, as well as well as by some Civilian Aviation Authority (CAA) ones. The reports typically include information such as dates, locations, weather, and altitude, plus detailed descriptions of appearance and movement. Some messages from the Military Air Transport Service (MATS) and Army Airways Communications System (AACS) are also included, as well as additional military intelligence reports, several diagrams, and a report from a weather station in Japan.

pdfs.title: 38_143685_box_Incident_Summaries_101-172
pdfs.blurb: Declassified Air Force intelligence compilation of unidentified flying object check-list incident reports numbered 101 through 172, covering sightings between February and roughly mid-1948 across the United States. Each entry follows a standardized RESTRICTED/CONFIDENTIAL form recording date, time, location, observer, object characteristics, and remarks, with supporting witness statements, correspondence (including speculative letters from Pueblo's Norman G. Markham and analysis by Dr. Lincoln LaPaz of the Institute of Meteoritics), and Fourth Air Force investigative memoranda signed by Lt. Col. Donald L. Springer. Notable incidents include the Norcatur, Kansas fireball of 18 February 1948, B-29 crew sightings near Green River Utah, the Bakersfield California flaming-object reports, Smyrna AAF observations, and the Baltimore foreign-aircraft sound report relayed via SAC Andrews.
pdfs.officialBlurb: Each of these incident summaries includes a "Check-List - Unidentified Flying Objects" that contains details about the incident. Many summaries also include witness lists or statements and other narrative reports or descriptions.

pdfs.title: 38_143685_box_Incident_Summaries_173-233
pdfs.blurb: Air Materiel Command (Wright-Patterson, Dayton) Project SIGN/GRUDGE-era Incident Summary sheets numbered 173 through 233, transmitted under Exhibit #529 dated 22 March 1949 and declassified under authority NND 917033. Each standardized form captures a separate UFO sighting with date/time, observer position, size/shape/color, altitude, speed, tactics, sound, weather, and an investigator narrative; included accounts feature E.B. Williams of United Lead Co. observing a cone-shaped white-hot object near New Orleans, A. Ruble Angier and John C. Fairchild reporting a circular silvery object over Santa Fe on 23 September 1948, and retired Army Col. Horace S. Eakins describing an undulating amoeba-shaped craft over Castro's Ranch, California. Originally classified SECRET; now stamped declassified.
pdfs.officialBlurb: Each of these incident summaries includes a "Check-List - Unidentified Flying Objects" that contains details about the incident. Many summaries also include witness lists or statements and other narrative reports or descriptions.

pdfs.title: 38_143685_box7_Incident_Summaries_1-100
pdfs.blurb: Declassified Department of War (Air Force) intelligence file from Box 7 (NND 917033) containing standardized CHECK-LIST UNIDENTIFIED FLYING OBJECTS incident summaries collected during the summer of 1947 and into early 1948, originally classified Confidential/Restricted. Each entry records observer name, occupation, location, time, altitude, speed, shape, color and reliability evaluation for sightings reported at sites such as Muroc Army Air Field, California (Lt. Joseph C. McHenry, Jannette Marie Scott, S/Sgt Joseph Ruvolo, Maj. Richard R. Shoop, Maj. J.C. Wise) and Rogers Dry Lake. Later pages cover the January 7, 1948 Godman Field, Kentucky event involving Col. Guy Hix and Capt. Thomas F. Mantell's fatal pursuit of an object in NG 869.
pdfs.officialBlurb: Each of these incident summaries includes a "Check-List - Unidentified Flying Objects" that contains details about the incident. Many summaries also include witness lists or statements and other narrative reports or descriptions.

pdfs.title: 59_214434_SP 16 [7.18.1963]
pdfs.blurb: National Aeronautics and Space Council memorandum dated July 18, 1963, marked OFFICIAL USE ONLY (declassified), from Maxwell W. Hunter II, Member of the Professional Staff, to Robert F. Packard at the Department of State's Office of International Scientific Affairs, subject Thoughts on the Space Alien Race Question. The six-page essay weighs scientific and theological views on extraterrestrial life, references the BNSP Task I deliberations, the canali of Mars, hot gases at the lunar crater Alphonsus, Lunik III and Mariner II findings, and Einstein's speed-of-light limitation, then sketches three contact scenarios (chemical-rocket Martians, near-light-speed colonizing race, faster-than-light civilization) and recommends rapidly identifying which type humanity faces should contact occur.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This memorandum, dated July 18, 1963, from the Executive Office of the President, National Aeronautics and Space Council, relates to thoughts on the space alien race question. Included are details relating to plans if alien intelligence is discovered, expanding scientific knowledge, the possibility of life on Mars, and diplomatic policy.

pdfs.blurb: Memorandum dated July 18, 1963 from Maxwell W. Hunter II, Member of the Professional Staff at the National Aeronautics and Space Council in the Executive Office of the President, to Robert F. Packard at the Department of State's Office of International Scientific Affairs, marked OFFICIAL USE ONLY and subsequently declassified. Titled 'Thoughts on the Space Alien Race Question,' the six-page essay surveys policy implications if intelligent extraterrestrial life were discovered, weighing prevailing astronomical theories of planetary formation, biological abiogenesis, the Martian Canali, hot gas emissions from the crater Alphonsus, and Lunik III imagery. Hunter classifies hypothetical aliens into three tiers - chemical-rocket Martians, near-light-speed nuclear travelers from population-pressured stellar civilizations, and faster-than-light visitors who would defeat Einsteinian physics - and concludes that any encounter would force an immediate burying of 'all Terrestrial hatchets,' though no preparation is likely until contact actually occurs.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This two page memorandum, dated July 18, 1952, relates to increased reports of unidentified flying objects (UFOs). Included in the record are possible explanations of increased sightings, such as technological improvements, historical records of UFOs, and U.S. Air Force opinions on UFOs.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-101634279_100-DE-18221_Serial_844
pdfs.blurb: FBI Detroit field office memorandum dated April 17, 1958 from Special Agent Robert Ross Reynolds to SAC Detroit (file 100-26505, 100-18221-894), a single-page report on subject Unidentified Flying Objects. Documents a 4:08 a.m. telephone call from David Weaver of 15801 Decosta, Detroit (KEnwood 8-7546), a 23-year-old son of a Detroit policeman with prior Civil Air Patrol experience, who reported observing a circular object with a crystal-type dome reflecting lights crossing the city three blocks south of Six Mile at Lamphere Street, traveling from southwest to north. Weaver had been unable to reach Selfridge Field; SA Reynolds recommended advising proper Air Force authorities. Document is fully unredacted apart from routine NARA screening notations.
pdfs.officialBlurb: An FBI memo from 1958 reporting a UFO sighting by a Detroit man who described a "circular object with a crystal-type dome," and recommending that the information be forwarded to "proper air force authorities."

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-101634279_100-DE-26505
pdfs.blurb: FBI Detroit field office file 100-DE-26505 documenting the November 7, 1957 interview of Wladyslaw "Walter" Krasuski of 5457 Joseph Campau, Detroit, by SAC Detroit at the urgent direction of Director Hoover after Krasuski wrote to Robert Cutler, Special Assistant to President Eisenhower, claiming knowledge of the "rocket in Texas." Krasuski, a Polish POW held by the Germans from May 1942 to 1945, recounted observing in 1944 near Gut Alt Golssen a circular vehicle 75-100 yards in diameter and 14 feet tall, with a propeller-like rotating middle section, rising vertically inside an SS-guarded tarpaulin enclosure while engines stalled and a high-pitched whining was heard. File also includes a 1966 letter from the Oklahoma UFO Research Association director Kerry Liesch and SAC Paul H. Stoddard's reply.
pdfs.officialBlurb: An FBI report from 1957 detailing the interview with Wladyslaw Krasuski, who recounted seeing a large, circular, vertically-rising vehicle in 1944 Germany near a German military compound.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_1
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 (Section 1, 185 pages, FOIPA #993087/#1311404), comprising the Bureau's earliest flying-disc correspondence and clippings from July-August 1947. Includes newspaper articles about Reverend Joseph Brasky's circular saw disc in Grafton, Wisconsin, public letters addressed to Director J. Edgar Hoover, an August 5, 1947 memo from Hoover to Colonel L.R. Forney of War Department Intelligence forwarding citizen Fred R. Reibold's report, internal teletypes between SAC offices (Milwaukee, New Orleans, Washington), and a Western Union telegram from inventor Woodson C. Cope. Declassified under FBI Automatic Declassification Guide of May 24, 2007.
pdfs.officialBlurb: The FBI's 62-HQ-83894 case file includes investigative records, eyewitness testimonies, and public reports concerning Unidentified Flying Objects and flying discs documented between June 1947 and July 1968. The records include high-profile incident accounts, photographic evidence from sites like Oak Ridge, TN, and technical proposals regarding potential propulsion systems. Additional topics include convention programs, researcher accounts, and extensive media coverage from the period.  This file is partially posted on FBI vault with more redactions and some pages missing.  Included here is the complete case file with several newly declassified pages and only minor redactions.

pdfs.title: 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_8
pdfs.blurb: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 8 (serials 344-384), a compilation of 1954-1957 internal memoranda, airtels, and Director Hoover correspondence on flying-saucer matters. Cincinnati SAC reports on Truman Bethurum, George Hunt Williamson, and promoter Henry Maday's Taft Auditorium lecture; WFO documents Navy Bureau of Aeronautics officer John Hutson's interviews with Frances Swan of South Berwick, Maine, who claimed thought-transmission contact with extraterrestrials 'AFFA' and 'PONNAR' aboard ships M-4 and L-11, joined by retired Adm. Herbert B. Knowles and Canadian physicist Wilbur B. Smith. Additional items include a Tucson form letter from London 'Aerial Phenomena Investigator' Mattan, the Detroit Flying Saucer Club inquiry into member Randall Cox, and Wladyslaw Krasuski's 1944 sighting near Gut Alt Golssen, Germany, forwarded to NSC's J. Patrick Coyne. Lightly redacted with FOIPA stamps.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D10, Mission Report, Middle East, May 2022
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP) from a 432 AEW ISR sortie over Iraq on 6 May 2022 under Operation INHERENT RESOLVE, declassified 7 October 2025 by MG Richard A. Harrison. The aircraft conducted target development and SIGINT collection from 0958Z to 2036Z near MGRS 38SMC, with full-motion video exploited by DGS-1. Between 1514Z and 1934Z the crew observed five UAP transiting the field of view; the first, with the visual signature of a possible missile, was the principal contact, while the remaining four matched the profile of birds. Dust hindered FMV ground collection. Names, unit identifiers, aircraft type, and coordinates redacted under (b)(3), 130b, (b)(6) and (b)(1)1.4a.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
This document is a Mission Report (MISREP), a standardized reporting form the U.S. Military uses to record the circumstances surrounding its operations. U.S. military services often use MISREPs to report Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) to AARO. The GENTEXT, or “general text” section of these reports often contains important qualitative, contextual information, distinguishing it from the more quantitative, or numerical, data found elsewhere in the report. 
A U.S. military operator reported observing “5x UAP fly across the screen.” The report continues by describing one of those observations as a “possible missile” and the remaining four as “possible birds.”
All descriptive and estimative language contained in this report reflects the reporter’s subjective interpretation at the time of the event. Such characterizations should not be interpreted as a conclusive indication of the presence or absence of any intrinsic object features or performance characteristics.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D12, Mission Report, Iraq, May 2022
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP undefined-7528881) from the 196th Attack Squadron under Operation Inherent Resolve, declassified by USCENTCOM Chief of Staff MG Richard A. Harrison on October 8, 2025 and released to AARO. The reconnaissance asset took off late from OKAS (Iraq) at 0542Z on May 20, 2022, conducted SIGINT via AIRHANDLER v2 and FMV in support of MAG, and at 2043Z observed a single UAP at grid 38S MC near 18,000 feet flying north to north-east; the screener could not get a positive ID and the asset followed as long as possible before recovering to OKAS. Heavily redacted under SECRET//REL TO USA, FVEY with 1.4a and (b)(3)/(b)(6) FOIA exemptions throughout.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D16, Mission Report, Syria, July 2022
pdfs.blurb: Secret//NOFORN MISREP undefined-7816710 from the 89th Attack Squadron, 432 AEW, covering a long-endurance MQ-9 ISR sortie out of Muwaffaq Salti AB (OJMS) for Task Force Chosin on 30-31 July 2022 (20.9 mission hours, 17.2 IMINT, 18.7 SIGINT). The narrative logs detailed surveillance of compounds, vehicle follows, and possible technicals around Syrian grid points 37SFU, including a convoy with poss-shooting-range activity and a pickup carrying a possible artillery system. At 310239Z the aircraft observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon near 37SFU2 in killbox 9 lasting under a minute moving north-to-south while the platform was at 19,359 ft and 116 KTS; full motion video was exploited by DGS-1. Declassified 8 October 2025 under MDR 25-0094.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D18, Mission Report, Iraq, December 2022
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM Operation INHERENT RESOLVE mission report (MISREP undefined-8216304) from the 482nd Attack Squadron, 20th Fighter Wing, covering a 19-hour 17-minute ISR flight that took off from OKAS at 1206Z on 1 December 2022 and recovered at OKAS at 0723Z on 2 December, declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison on 8 October 2025 under MDR 25-0094 thru 25-0099. At 1620Z the crew observed one possible UAP/UAV flying west to east near grid 38S MB at flight level 180; the asset did not break tasking to follow it. Names, tail numbers, and grid coordinates redacted under FOIA (b)(3) and (b)(6).

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D19, Mission Report, Syria, February 21, 2023
pdfs.blurb: Secret//NOFORN USAF mission report (MISREP undefined-8353978) submitted by the 389th Expeditionary Fighter Squadron, 332 AEW, documenting a two-ship F-15E Defensive Counter-Air sortie from Muwaffaq Salti Air Base (OJMS), Jordan in support of Operation Inherent Resolve on 20-21 February 2023. While on station near ESSA the flight experienced multi-frequency radar jamming on the APG-82 (8.8-9.9 GHz) attributed to a suspected Turkish X-band emitter, then at 0025Z observed three possible UAP near Shaddadi at FL240 (white objects, IR-significant, no radar return, no health effects) and at 0135Z a single possible balloon at FL210; weapons-system video was produced for both events. The report logs three air refuelings on MOM and GANDER tracks, full crew/aircraft/munitions configuration, and was declassified 8 October 2025 under MDR 25-0094.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D20, Mission Report, Southern United States, 2020
pdfs.blurb: MISREP (Mission Report) undefined-8584059 from the 77th Expeditionary Fighter Squadron, 378 AEW, documents a 31 March 2023 two-ship F-16CM Defensive Counter Air sortie launched from Prince Sultan Air Base (OEPS) in support of Operation Inherent Resolve, controlled by JTAC callsign KINGPIN. At 2302:05Z over the ESSA killbox northeast of RLZ in Syrian airspace, the flight observed approximately 10 to 20 bright objects maneuvering quickly west to east at FL600+; the targeting pod (TV mode) held each object for roughly 20 seconds before it dimmed, and a comparison between a possible UAP and a star produced different results. Originally classified SECRET//NOFORN with a 2048 declassification date, the document was declassified 8 October 2025 by MG Richard A. Harrison, USCENTCOM Chief of Staff (MDR 25-0094 through 25-0099), and contains FOIA (b)(3), (b)(6) and 1.4(a)/1.4(g) redactions to aircrew names, callsigns, and tail numbers.

A U.S. military operator reported observing “several bright objects maneuvering quickly west to east northeast. The operator reported achieving a track on the UAP via an onboard targeting pod for approximately 20 seconds. The report describes that UAP then dimmed and disappeared from the targeting pod.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D23, Mission Report, United Arab Emirates, October 2023
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 9319618, USCENTCOM MDR 25-0104) declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison on 12 September 2025 documenting a 24 October 2023 ISR sortie by a 50th Attack Squadron / 432 AEW aircraft (callsign masked) flown out of Al Dhafra (OMAM) in support of NAVCENT under Operation Spartan Shield. The mission ran a 20-hour-43-minute sortie over the Persian Gulf, collected SIGINT via AIRHANDLER and IMINT with ANDAS1, and at 0145Z received a professional guard call from Iranian Air Defense ordering it to maintain distance from the border. At 0241Z and again at 0322Z the crew observed two separate UAPs in MGRS grid 39RXK6/39RYJ, solid and thermally cold, tracked at roughly 320 mph and 440 mph at FL243 - both assessed as benign with footage exploited by DGS-2.

pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM Mission Report (MISREP undefined-9629373) from the 33 SOS originator, classified SECRET//REL TO USA, FVEY and declassified 24 October 2025 by MG Richard A. Harrison. Records an AFSOC ISR sortie taking off 0109Z 25 January 2024 from LGLR (Larissa, Greece) supporting tasking ATO 24-024 mission 4055, with FMV/SIGINT collection against a tasked area at 36RXV19 including a stop-follow on a silver SUV and observation of a large white box transferring from a black SUV to a white van. Harrison. The 10-hour 13-minute ISR sortie, tasked through the 609 CAOC, included two non-interference-basis dhow-scanning tasks in support of JTAC ITC. Harrison on 24 October 2025, documenting a 20 September 2024 Operation INHERENT RESOLVE armed-overwatch sortie by a 16 SOS / 27 SOW aircraft (platform masked under 1.4a) flying out of OKAS to ROZ Raindrop at Ayn Al Asad Airbase, Iraq. During a weapons calibration in which the crew expended 20x105mm, 101x30mm and one AGM-176 Griffin, the WSO and CSO observed at 2027Z an unidentified solid object fly through the MX-20 and MX-25 sensor field of view at high speed between munition release and impact, generating an IR lens flare from a significant heat source; it was unclear whether a smaller object detached from the primary UAP before exiting frame, and the crew did not reacquire it.

pdfs.blurb: AFCENT mission report (Misrep 8799515) submitted as a SECRET MISREP from a redacted USAF squadron operating in the Arabian Gulf area of operations, with a 2048 declassification date. Most administrative fields including operation name, callsign, tail numbers, takeoff/landing locations, times, and personnel POCs are withheld under (b)(1) 1.4a and (b)(6)/(b)(3) 10 U.S.C. 130b. The substantive UAP narrative records that at approximately 1736Z the crew observed four UAP beneath a redacted altitude reference: one at 17:36:22, two side-by-side at 17:36:30, and one at 17:36:49, with cloud coverage preventing the sensor from following or obtaining a clear visual. Heavily redacted throughout.

A U.S. military operator reported observing a “line of dots followed by a trailing dot.”

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D33, Mission Report, Greece, October 2023
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 9329374) from the 33rd Special Operations Squadron documenting a 13-hour 30-minute ISR sortie launched from LGLR (Larissa, Greece) on 26-27 October 2023 and recovered at OJMS (Amman, Jordan), declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison on 22 January 2026. The aircraft, equipped with AN/DAS-4 targeting pod, AWGMESH, and Link 16, conducted FMV/SIGINT collection in support of a masked tasking under the Violent Extremist Group global campaign plan. At 0035Z on 27 October the crew observed a single circular UAP flying just above ocean water, executing sharp 90-degree turns at an estimated 80 mph before being lost from the feed at 0038Z; assessed benign with unknown propulsion. Names and tasking specifics redacted.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D38, Range Fouler Debrief, Middle East, May 2020
pdfs.blurb: Range Fouler Debrief Form submitted to SPEAR documenting an ISR aircrew observation on 14 May 2020 at 20:40Z (night) at coordinates 28:31:4N 49:52:4E, altitude 20,000 feet, in the Persian Gulf. While performing an ISR tasking using ULTN/Black Hot/Lin sensors, the crew tracked a single solid white round object flying through the field of view; the object was briefly lost and reacquired, appeared to make erratic movements just above the water, and was followed at 4x zoom until track placement was lost. Radar showed an intermittent trackfile with no EA indications, AIM-9x self-track, ATFLIR autotrack or tally. Aircrew names, squadron, and contact details redacted under (b)(6); declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison.

pdfs.blurb: Range Fouler Debrief Form filed by an O-2 pilot of the 482 ATKS for an ISR mission on 31 August 2020 at dusk, recording a contact at 118,000 feet altitude with constant altitude, winds 150/230, on MGRS grid 39RWL (partially redacted). The narrative describes an initial object tracked across the sensor screen, then 'surpassed by another object of same size and shape but much higher speed,' with three objects appearing simultaneously and moving among each other; the radar was 'Other,' with no stable trackfile, AIM-9X self-track, ATFLIR autotrack, or tally achieved, and 'Other Shape' with apparent propulsion checked. Released through USCENTCOM MDR 26-0028 (FOUO/PA applies) with FOIA (b)(6) and 1.4(a) redactions; the OCR text contains garbled characters in places.

Harrison. The aircrew reports a single round, cold (in IR) contact tracked for about one minute at MGRS 40Q BD with a redacted lat/long, traveling 319 degrees at roughly 20 mph and making several abrupt directional changes; the sensor was aimed about 50 degrees below the aircraft's 19,073 ft altitude with 4.06 NM slant range. With IR set to black-hot the object appeared bright white and was characterized as round, opaque, and reflective, with no apparent propulsion, no markings, and no stable radar trackfile or AIM-9X self-track.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D48, Department of the Air Force Report, 1996
pdfs.blurb: Final technical report (RTI/5180/77-43F, dated September 10, 1996) prepared by James A. Ward, Jr. and Robert M. Montgomery of Research Triangle Institute under contract F04703-91-C-0112 for the Air Force's 30th and 45th Space Wings (Vandenberg AFB and Patrick AFB). The 180-page study models Mode-5 unlikely failure responses for space-booster launch risk analysis using RTI's DAMP program, deriving shaping constants and failure probabilities for Atlas, Delta, Titan, Thor, and LLV1 launch vehicles. An extensive appendix narrates failure histories for each vehicle program through August 1996. Distribution-restricted to U.S. government agencies and contractors.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This report describes the Modeling of Unlikely Space-Booster Failures in Risk Calculations, documenting historical launch failure modes and recommending corrective actions to address them using novel modelling techniques.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D49, Launch Summary, Vandenberg AFB, 2000
pdfs.blurb: Vandenberg AFB Launch Summary 1958-2000, the official chronological registry compiled by the 30th Space Wing Office of History (Jeffrey Geiger) as of 3 February 2000. The 113-page reference catalogs every major launch from Vandenberg beginning 16 December 1958, organized by booster and command, and includes a Launch Facility Guide cross-referencing building numbers (e.g., SLC-4E, SLC-6, LF-21) and a multi-page glossary of program acronyms (ABRES, ICBM, DMSP, NAVSTAR, Peacekeeper DT&E phases, Athena, etc.). Distribution covers HQ AFSPC, USSPACECOM, NASA, JPL, Aerospace Corporation, Boeing, TRW, and Orbital Sciences. Fully unclassified administrative reference document with no UAP narrative content.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This report summarizes the historical record of launches occurring at Vandenberg Air Force Base between 1958 and 2000.

AFOSI agrees to handle the request as a derivative classification review rather than a full declassification (which would require AFOSI Commander signature) and approves use of the summary at the UNCLASSIFIED level. pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D55, Mission Report, Syria, November 2016
pdfs.blurb: Brief one-page mission report declassified by USCENTCOM Chief of Staff MG Richard A. Harrison covering a U.S. Navy P-8A Poseidon observation on 18 November 2016 while monitoring Russian Carrier Task Group activity in the Eastern Mediterranean. At 1310Z the aircraft's EO/IR sensor detected a possible sea-skimming missile near a redacted origin traveling at approximately 500 KTS on a southeasterly heading outbound from the KCTG for about two minutes before visual contact was lost at 1312Z roughly 40 NM northwest of Latakia, with the object passing between the Russian INGUL ARS and an unidentified vessel. CTG 67.1 commentary by the mission commander assessed the interaction as safe and consistent with standard KCTG activity; weather was clear with no range limitations.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D56, Range Fouler Debrief, Arabian Sea, August 2020
pdfs.blurb: Range Fouler Debrief Form completed by an O-3 pilot of HSM-73 documenting a 24 August 2020 night observation over the North Arabian Sea (working area W-72 1A, side number 705, BuNo 168122) at 00:04:30Z during routine SSC operations at 21,440 feet and 310 knots. The aviator reported three small unidentified air contacts on a westerly heading with no electronic support, radar track, or IFF returns; one was tracked visually before disappearing behind a cloud, after which two additional contacts appeared to its east, all maintaining relative course, speed, and altitude. The form notes round shape with wings/airframe, metallic and reflective characteristics, no stable trackfile and no AIM-9X self-track or ATFLIR autotrack. Released under USCENTCOM MDR 26-0038 to 26-0046 with FOIA (b)(6) redactions to identifying contact information.

Upon regaining contact, the operator reported observing two additional UAP to the east of the first. pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D57, Range Fouler Reporting Form, Gulf of Aden, September 2020

Documents a nighttime ISR contact on 04 September 2020 from 21:09Z to 21:17Z over the Gulf of Aden at MGRS grid 38P, while the aircraft orbited at 23,819 feet HAT. The crew tracked via IR sensor (set to black-hot) a single round, cold object showing as bright white, traveling 168 degrees at 277 mph with the sensor aimed 39 degrees below own altitude (slant range 6.1 NM, ground range 8.81 km); the contact made several abrupt directional changes over the eight-minute interaction. The form notes the object was round, opaque, with no visible markings, wings, moving parts or apparent propulsion.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D61, Mission Report, Persian Gulf, August 2020
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM Mission Report (MISREP 4685903) from the 482 ATKS, 432 AEW, declassified 22 January 2026 by MG Richard A. Harrison. Documents an AREC ISR sortie taking off from OKAS at 2307Z 26 August 2020 supporting NAVCENT operations over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman, conducting AN/DAS-4 FMV scans of IRIN/IRGCN vessels and observing a docked vessel at Greater Tunb naval port, a probable Naser WPB alongside a merchant vessel dead in the water, and a possible Il-76 Candid at Abu Musa Island airfield. At 0532Z the aircraft received a professional Iranian Air Defense guard call; from 1527Z to 1529Z near grid 39RVM8 the crew tracked a formation of unidentified flying objects traveling NE-NW along the coast for about two minutes before light cloud cover defeated positive identification.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D62, Mission Report, Strait of Hormuz, September 2020
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 4782130, USCENTCOM MDR 26-0019) declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison on 22 January 2026 covering a 15-16 September 2020 AREC sortie by a 482nd Attack Squadron / 432 AEW aircraft from OKAS supporting NAVCENT in the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman. The 20.9-hour mission logged 15.2 IMINT and 19.3 SIGINT hours via AIRHANDLER and ANDAS4, observed an IRIN SA-5 launcher near Abu Musa Island and two probable Houdong vessels at piers, and received three guard calls from Iranian Air Defense (two directive, one professional). The aircraft also experienced two unknown EMI lost-link events totaling 38 minutes and at 1732Z observed a UAP near MGRS 39RVM5; FMV was exploited by DGS1.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D7, Mission Report, Arabian Gulf, 2020
pdfs.blurb: Heavily redacted six-page mission report (the first five pages contain only 1.4(a) classification markings with no readable content) covering a 2020 sortie in which an aircrew tracked a UAP via radar and visually identified it on Targeting FLIR. The single legible GENTEXT/UAP block describes the object as resembling a balloon similar to a previously reported UAP from the 48th Fighter Wing, traveling with the winds at 31,000 ft MSL near grid 323'S; the platform achieved a Weapons Quality 1 track and was able to make a next-to-shoot on the contact while visually identifying it in the TFLIR. Effectively a near-fully-redacted form with a short narrative confirming the balloon-like classification.

pdfs.title: DOW-UAP-D74, Mission Report, Syria, November 2023
pdfs.blurb: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 9381202, USCENTCOM MDR 25-0072) classified SECRET//NOFORN and recommended for release to AARO by USCENTCOM Chief of Staff MG Brandon R. Tegtmeier on 2 June 2025. The report documents a 9 November 2023 ISR sortie under Operation INHERENT RESOLVE by a 609 CAOC-controlled Air Force aircraft conducting FMV/SIGINT collection over MGRS grids in Syria; the aircraft callsign, tail number and unit are masked under 1.4a. While returning to base at 2153Z, the crew observed a probable UAP shaped like a 'bouncy ball' that approached from the south at near co-altitude, descended past the aircraft and tracked roughly 424 knots for about seven minutes before going out of range, with no emissions, no aircrew effects, and an observer assessment of benign.

pdfs.officialBlurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of a still image derived from a U.S. military system in 2022. The original reporter digitally altered the imagery by adding a red line encircling an area of interest before submitting it to AARO. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D12, described the UAP as moving from north to northeast. The operator reported that they were unable to positively identify the UAP.

Image Description: The image contains an encircled, elongated area of contrast in the top left quarter. The area of contrast increases in intensity along its length from top left to bottom right.

This image description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.
