pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969
pdfs.blurb: Pages 742, 743, 748, and 778 of the Apollo 12 air-to-ground voice transcript (Tape 90/3, 90/4, 90/9, 93/8) capture exchanges between CapCom (CC), Commander/LMP aboard the Lunar Module 'Intrepid' (Pete Conrad and Alan Bean), and Command Module Pilot Dick Gordon aboard 'Yankee Clipper' at mission elapsed times around 5d19h-6d00h, covering CSM state-vector and RLS uplinks, lift-off time 142:03:47, P22 tracking PAD data, and a battery-charge termination at LOS. The CDR-LM describes looking through the AOT in the dark quadrant and seeing 'particles of light, flashes of light' that appear to be 'escaping the Moon' and pressing off toward the stars; CapCom and TRW attribute an all-eights pulsing display on the AGS DEDA to EMI seen during Bethpage testing. A later passage notes the LM tracking light appears burned out, though Houston's electrical readings indicate current is still flowing. The transcript is fully unredacted.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Apollo 12 was the fourth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon and the second to land astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 12 Technical Air-to-Ground Voice Transcription, November 1969, highlighting two periods in which astronauts reported observing unidentified phenomenon: a one hour period on the fifth day, and a two minute period on the sixth day. These transcripts contain contemporaneous observations by the flight crew reacting to unidentified phenomenon.
•	Day 05, Hour 19, Minute 14, Second 58 through Day 05, Hour 20, Minute 12, Second 14:
o	At 05:19:27:25, the pilot of the Lunar Module (LMP-LM), Astronaut Alan L. Bean, described observing particles and flashes of light “sailing off in space” via the onboard Alignment Optical Telescope (AOT). He characterized these phenomenon as “escaping the Moon.”
•	Day 06, Hour 00, Minute 21, Second 42 through Day 06, Hour 00, Minute 23, Second 33:
o	Mission Commander, Charles “Pete” Conrad, described observing floating debris outside the lunar module, which had been illuminated by the module’s onboard tracking light. At 06:00:21:51, Conrad assessed that the tracking light had burnt out because he could no longer see the debris from the module.

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D2, Apollo 17 Transcript, 1972
pdfs.blurb: NASA Apollo 17 air-to-ground voice transcription (Tapes 5, 46, 47, 59, and 60) covering translunar coast and lunar-orbit conversations in December 1972 between CapComs 'Bob' and 'Gordy' and the crew CDR Eugene Cernan, CMP Ronald Evans and LMP Harrison 'Jack' Schmitt. The crew describe bright tumbling fragments after S-IVB separation, ALFMED cosmic-ray light-flash observations Cernan reports seeing while trying to sleep, and a rotating 'flasher' object Cernan and Schmitt eventually agree is the spent S-IVB stage glinting end-on with engine bell visible. Schmitt also delivers continuous weather commentary on Pacific cyclones near the Solomons and Hawaii and, near Grimaldi crater, reports a bright flash on the lunar surface and asks Houston to check the seismometers.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Apollo 17 was the ninth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon, and the sixth to land astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 17 Technical Air-to-Ground Voice Transcription, December 1972, highlighting three periods in which astronauts reported observing unidentified phenomenon: a nine minute period on the first day, a three hour period on the second day, and a six minute period on the third day. 
•	Day 00, Hour 03, Minute 34, Second 10 through Day 00, Hour 03, Minute 42, Second 29:
    o	Command Module Pilot (CMP), Ronald Evans, reported observing “very bright particles or fragments” drifting and “tumbling” near the spacecraft as it maneuvered. Lunar Module Pilot (LMP), Harrison “Jack” Schmitt, described the phenomenon as looking “like the Fourth of July.” The astronauts speculated that the phenomenon may be attributable to ice or paint fragments dislodging from a separated component of the spacecraft (S-IVB) but characterized that assessment as a “wild guess.”
•	Day 02, Hour 18, Minute 42, Second 34 through Day 02, Hour 21, Minute 07, Second 05:
    o	Mission Commander, Eugene A. Cernan, reported difficulty sleeping and described having observed “some sets of the streaks.” He also described an intense light flashing between his eyes, describing its intensity as comparable to that of a train headlight and characterizing it as “imposing.” Over the next three hours, Cernan described observing several flashing, rotating phenomenon that he assessed as corresponding to physical objects in space rather than a purely optical phenomenon. LMP Schmitt also reported observing similar phenomenon, though he again assessed the source of his observation to be a separated rocket stage (S-IVB). At 02:20:55:22, Cernan reported observing two additional distant flashing objects, though he assessed them as Spacecraft/Lunar Module Adapter panels (SLA panel), another separated component of the Saturn V rocket.
•	Day 03, Hour 15, Minute 33, Second 25 through Day 03, Hour 15, Minute 39, Second 46:
    o	At 03:15:38:09, LMP Schmitt exclaimed that he had observed a flash on the lunar surface north of Grimaldi (crater).

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969
pdfs.blurb: Excerpts from the NASA Manned Spacecraft Center Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing dated 31 July 1969, prepared by the Mission Operations Branch, Flight Crew Support Division (originally Confidential, downgraded after 12 years), Volumes I and II. The transcribed dialogue between astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins covers their translunar sighting of an unidentified L- or open-suitcase-shaped object that appeared cylindrical through the sextant, which they tentatively associated with the S-IVB stage 6,000 miles distant; Aldrin's repeated observations of paired flashes inside the cabin during sleep periods believed possibly caused by cosmic-ray penetration; and a bright light source observed during lunar orbit and transearth coast initially thought to be a laser but later assessed as sunlight glinting off a body of water.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Apollo 11 was the third crewed mission to the Moon and the first to land Astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing (Volumes 1 and 2) from July 31, 1969. The document highlights three observations: one, an object on the way out to the Moon; two, flashes of light inside the cabin; and three, a sighting on the return trip of a bright light tentatively assumed by the crew to be a laser.

•	Page 6-33 (Vol. 1). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin]: “The first unusual thing that we saw I guess was 1 day out or something pretty close to the moon. It had a sizeable dimension to it, so we put the monocular on it.” The crew speculated that it could have been the S-IVB stage of the Saturn V launch vehicle.
•	Page 6-37 (Vol. 1). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin] “The other observation that I made accumulated gradually. I don’t know whether I saw it the first night, but I’m sure I saw it the second night. I was trying to go to sleep with all the lights out. I observed what I thought were little flashes inside the cabin, spaced a couple of minutes apart…”
•	Page 21-1 (Vol. 2). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin] “I observed what appeared to be a fairly bright light source which we tentatively ascribed to a possible laser.”

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973
pdfs.blurb: Excerpt from NASA MSC-07632, the Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science dated 8 January 1973, prepared by the Planetary and Earth Sciences Division at the Manned Spacecraft Center, Houston. The reproduced pages contain remarks by a speaker identified as Henry discussing scientific results from the mission's astronomical observations, including the search for red-shifted Lyman-alpha radiation from ionized hydrogen in the Coma cluster, an unexpected ultraviolet background measured at high galactic latitudes north and south, OGO-5 follow-up Lyman-alpha hydrogen data interpreted by Gary Thomas of the University of Colorado as interstellar hydrogen streaming through the solar system, and observations of Earth's spectrum from outside the atmosphere.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Apollo 17 was the ninth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon, and the sixth to land Astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science on January 8, 1973, in which Dick Henry, co-investigator on the ultraviolet experiment on Apollo 17, discusses seeing results that were unexpected. 
•	Pages 119-120. “One of the most exciting results of X-ray astronomy was the fact that an X-ray background was observed over the sky that nobody had expected, and part of this is the gamma-ray background that Dr. Trombka talked about. In the UV, nobody knows, but you never know until you look. You do have to deal with this background of stars that we know is there. So, we did look at a large number of different points at high galactic latitudes, both north and south. The spectrum that we see is above this dark count. In other words, this abnormally high dark current did not, in fact, interfere with that experiment. The spectrum that we see looks like the spectrum of the hot star; however, we know that there were no hot stars within our field of view. Therefore, the most conservative interpretation, I think, is that what we're seeing is light from hot stars in the galactic plane going up out of the plane and reflecting off interstellar dust. There are certain characteristics of the spectrum, though, that don't fit that theory, and it's at least possible that this is extragalactic radiation. I'm looking forward very much to the detailed computer study of this, but it's going to take a long time.”

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D6, Apollo 17 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1973
pdfs.blurb: NASA Manned Spacecraft Center Apollo 17 Technical Crew Debriefing (MSC-07631) prepared by the Training Office, Crew Training and Simulation Division, Houston, Texas, dated 4 January 1973 and declassified under E.O. 13526 Sec 3.3(a) per the NASA Declassification Guide on 5/6/2026. The excerpted page records crew remarks by Command Module Pilot Ron Evans, Commander Gene Cernan, and Lunar Module Pilot Harrison Schmitt covering a tunnel-with-bright-spot view through the rendezvous window after a fireball, a quip about an aircraft carrier sighting during recovery, and Schmitt's account of nearly continuous transearth light flashes when dark-adapted, including one possible flash on the lunar surface and the suppression of flashes during the ALFMED blindfold experiment.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Apollo 17 was the ninth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon, and the sixth to land Astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 17 Technical Crew Debriefing on January 4, 1973, in which astronaut Harrison Schmitt reported seeing light flashes.
•	Page 24-4. [Lunar Module Pilot Harrison Schmitt]: “We had light flashes just about continuously during the whole flight when we were dark adapted. I had one which I thought was a flash on the lunar surface. That one period of time when we had the blindfolds on for the ALFMED [Apollo Light Flash Moving Emulsion Detector] experiment there were just no visible flashes, although that evening, that night, before I went to sleep, I noticed that I was seeing the light flashes again.”

pdfs.title: NASA-UAP-D7, Skylab Techincal Crew Debriefing 1973
pdfs.blurb: NASA Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center Technical Crew Debriefings JSC-08053 (Skylab 1/2, June 30, 1973), JSC-08478 (Skylab 1/3, October 4, 1973) and JSC-08809 (Skylab 1/4, February 22, 1974, Part 1), prepared by the Crew Training and Simulation Division. Astronauts Conrad, Kerwin and Weitz discuss cosmic-ray light flashes seen with eyes closed; Bean, Lousma and Garriott describe a bright reddish rotating object tracked through the wardroom window for roughly 10 minutes about a week before splashdown in an orbit close to Skylab's, with a 10-second brightness period; Carr, Pogue and Gibson recount further sightings of objects moving relative to Skylab and unrelated technical issues including ammonia odors and OWS heat exchanger lint.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
Launched on May 14, 1973, Skylab was the United States’ first laboratory in space. From 1973 to 1974, the station was visited by three crews. This document contains excerpts from all three crews to visit the station. In the first excerpt taken from Skylab 1/2 [first crew] Technical Debriefing from June 30, 1973, highlights crew observations of light flashes. The second excerpt taken from Skylab 1/3 Technical Crew Debriefing from October 4, 1973, highlights two observations—a satellite in similar orbit and another object with a “reddish hue to it.” The final excerpt taken from the Skylab 1/4 Technical Crew Debriefing from February 22, 1974, highlights an observation of flashing lights outside Skylab.
•	Skylab 2 crew observation: 
o	Page 23-20. [Science Pilot for Skylab 2, Joesph Kerwin] “We saw light flashes. I think all of us saw them. I saw them most often when I was in the sack at night with my eyes closed but awake naturally. They tended to wax and wane in frequency.”
•	Skylab 3 crew observations: 
o	Page 7-4. [Science Pilot for Skylab 3, Owen Garriott] “We saw that satellite about a week before splashdown. That was one of the most unusual things that we saw and I guess Jack [Lousma] noticed it looking out the window. This bright reddish object was out there and we tracked it for about 5 or 10 minutes. It was obviously a satellite in a very similar orbit to our own.”
o	Page 20-1. [Science Pilot for Skylab 3, Owen Garriott] “Jack [Lousma] first noticed this rather large red star out the wardroom window. Upon close examination, it was much brighter than Jupiter or any of the other planets. It had a reddish hue to it, even though it was well above the horizon.”
•	Skylab 4 crew observation 
o	Page 7-8. [Commander for Skylab 4, Gerald P. Carr] “One other area of unusual events that we reported on the dump tapes was that on occasion we saw some lights flashing outside with very a definite motion relative to ours. We presumed that they were other pieces of Skylab, or possibly other satellites.”

pdfs.title: State Department UAP Cable 1, Papua New Guinea, January 28, 1985
pdfs.blurb: State Department diplomatic cable (MRN 85 PORT MORESBY 199, DTG 280653Z JAN 85) from AMEMBASSY PORT MORESBY signed Gardner, addressed to USCINCPAC Honolulu and 43rd Strategic Wing at Andersen AFB Guam with info copies to SECSTATE, Canberra, and Jakarta. The Limited Official Use telegram relays a 28 January 1985 informal inquiry from PNG's National Intelligence Organization regarding 24 January high-altitude, high-speed overflights that frightened residents in Wewak and prompted a public meeting attended by the Prime Minister. Reports include an Air Niugini pilot's radar contact over Angoram and visual contrails seen across PNG; embassy advised NIO it knew of no U.S. or B-52 flights in PNG airspace and requested confirmation. Released in full.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
This document is a U.S. Department of State diplomatic cable from the U.S. Embassy in Port Moresby, Papua New Guinea to USCINCPAC (United States Indo-Pacific Command) at Honolulu, HI on January 28, 1985. 
The cable reports that the U.S. Embassy to Papua New Guinea received an inquiry from the host nation’s intelligence services regarding reports of high-altitude, high-speed aircraft in Papua New Guinean airspace on the evening of January 24, 1985. The cable refers to a representative of the local intelligence services as “NIO,” or National Intelligence Officer, throughout. The NIO relayed to U.S. diplomatic personnel that residents had been “frightened by overflights, which led to the provincial premier’s calling of a public meeting on the subject.” The NIO also stated there had been “various reports of unidentified aerial phenomena the night of January 24, including fast-moving objects with lights, contrails, and noise.” The NIO assessed these reports as credible based upon the testimony of an Air Niugini pilot who said that their radar had “picked up aircraft flying south to north at high altitude and high speed.”
The cable concludes by characterizing the information provided by the NIO as “very sketchy.” It also sought clarification from U.S. INDOPACOM on the presence or absence of U.S. military aircraft within Papua New Guinean airspace on the night in question.

pdfs.title: State Department UAP Cable 2, Kazakhstan, January 31, 1994
pdfs.blurb: Unclassified State Department cable 94 DUSHANBE 259, dated 31 January 1994 from AMEMBASSY DUSHANBE (signed Escudero) to SECSTATE WASHDC with info copies to AMEMBASSY MOSCOW, TASHKENT, ASHGABAT, ALMATY, BEIJING, BISHKEK, CIA and DIA. Reports that on 27 January 1994 Tajik Air Chief Pilot AmCit Ed Rhodes and two American colleagues, flying a Boeing 747SP at 41,000 feet over Kazakhstan (45N/55E), observed a brilliant high-altitude object for roughly forty minutes performing circles, corkscrews and 90-degree turns at high G; Rhodes photographed it with a pocket Olympus camera, later flew under contrails estimated at 100,000 feet, and assessed the object as extraterrestrial and under intelligent control. Embassy comment offers no opinion. Released in full 25 February 2026.
pdfs.officialBlurb:
This document is a U.S. Department of State diplomatic cable from the U.S. Embassy in Dushanbe, Tajikistan to the Secretary of State in Washington, D.C. on January 31, 1994.
On January 27, 1994 one Tajik pilot and three American citizens encountered an UAP flying a 747 jet at 41,000 feet over Kazakhstan.  Object was a bright light of enormous intensity and approached over the horizon to the east at great speed and a much higher altitude.  Several pictures were taken of the craft making 90 degree turns, doing corkscrews and maneuvering in circles a great rates of speed.  Object was reported as resembling a bullet in flight.  Visual estimation of the contrails were at 100,000 feet, which was too high to leave contrails by ordinary aircraft.

pdfs.title: State Department UAP Cable 3, Tbilisi, Georgia, October 30, 2001
pdfs.blurb: Confidential U.S. Department of State cable 01 MOSCOW 13169 dated 30 October 2001 from AMEMBASSY MOSCOW (signed Ambassador Vershbow, classified by POLMINCOUNS George Krol) to SECSTATE and posts including Tbilisi, USOSCE, SECDEF, and Joint Staff. The cable, titled UFOs Over Georgia: Strange Encounters of an MFA Kind, reports Deputy Foreign Minister Mamedov's and MFA Georgia desk chief Tereoken's denials of Russian aircraft involvement in the 28-29 October 2001 Kodori Gorge bombings alleged by Tbilisi, with Tereoken quipping that reports of planes might as well have been about UFOs. The remainder addresses the partial Russian withdrawal from Gudauta base, Speaker Zurab Zvania's Moscow visit, possible Ivanov travel to Tbilisi, and a planned Shevardnadze-Putin meeting at the CIS Summit. Released in full.
pdfs.officialBlurb: On October 28-29, there was an incident alleged by the Georgian Foreign Ministry that Russian aircraft had violated Georgian airspace and bombed areas of the Kodori Gorge.  Russians denied any of the claims and said that it could have been UFOs.  Cable authors note that Russians typically engage in the “bold lie” when they wish to conceal actions.

pdfs.title: State Department UAP Cable 4, Ashgabat, Turkmenistan, November 5, 2004
pdfs.blurb: Unclassified State Department cable 04 ASHGABAT 1028 dated 12 November 2004 from AMEMBASSY ASHGABAT, signed by Charge Jacobson, sent action to SECSTATE WASHDC and info to USOSCE, USEU Brussels, CIA, DIA, NSC, USCENTCOM, SECDEF and the Joint Staff (TAGS AORC, TSPA, PREL, PGOV, EAID, OSCI, TX). The cable reports a 5 November 2004 meeting between the DCM and USAID Director and the board of the Union of UFOlogists (UOU) of Turkmenabat, Lebap Welayet, headed by President Ovezberdy Muradov - the first NGO to register after independence and the first to re-register under the austere 2003 NGO law. Despite its ostensible mission of studying extraterrestrial life, the UOU functions as an umbrella civil-society NGO of over 1,000 members assisting small-business registration, distributing CHAP humanitarian aid via Counterpart International to Tajik and Afghan refugees, and seeking $15,000 and $30,000 USAID grants for capacity building and to launch a 999-copy independent newsletter; Jacobson endorses it as 'crazy like a fox' and worthy of USG support.
pdfs.officialBlurb: UFOlogists of Turkmenistan has gained a positive reputation as a reliable partner for the United States in Turkmenistan to the bemusement of the cable’s author in the build up of civil society organizations within the country.  The reputation has become earned because everyone in Turkmenistan, apparently, “is interested in UFOs.”

pdfs.title: State Department UAP Cable 5, Mexico, September 16, 2003
pdfs.blurb: Unclassified U.S. Department of State diplomatic cable 23 MEXICO 2544 dated 16 September 2023 from AMEMBASSY MEXICO (signed Salazar, drafted by Sergio A. Moreno) to SECSTATE Washington and a wide intelligence-community distribution, titled Mexico: Weekly Political Blotter, Sep 11-15. The cable summarizes Mexican political developments including Marcelo Ebrard's challenge to MORENA, INE commission appointments, Omar Garcia Harfuch's resignation as Mexico City Security Secretary and replacement by Pablo Vazquez, Ricardo Monreal's withdrawal, attacks on Guerrero prosecutors, and Pablo Lemus's Jalisco gubernatorial launch. The final paragraph reports the 12 September 2023 Mexican Congressional hearing on UAP featuring journalist Jaime Maussan and former U.S. Navy pilot Ryan Graves, where Maussan presented two alleged alien corpses subsequently dismissed by Graves as an unsubstantiated stunt. Released in full.
pdfs.officialBlurb: On September 12, 20023 the Mexican Congress heard testimony on UAP from experts related to the debate about an Aerial Space Protection Law, which, if approved, would make Mexico the first country to formally acknowledge the presence of alien life on earth. Experts asked legislators to recognize UAP, guarantee airspace security, and allow UAP to be studied.  They presented to alleged alien corpses and videos of Mexican pilot’s encounters with fast-moving flying objects during flight. Disagreement about the efficacy and validity of the purported alien corpses.

pdfs.title: USPER Statement about UAP Sighting
pdfs.blurb: Heavily redacted Secret//NOFORN U.S. Person eyewitness statement from Witness 1 (a senior US intelligence official) describing an aerial search conducted in late 2025 with state partner helicopter Call Sign 1 and federal partners over a redacted mountain range west of a coded site, prompted by earlier orb/light reports and audible thuds correlated with a same-day office test. Beginning at 1751 hours and continuing past 2316 hours, pilots using NVG and FLIR and Witness 1 with the naked eye observed orbs flaring up in horizontal formations, a swarm of lights, two large orange-and-white oval orbs that appeared near the helicopter rotor disk, and orbs apparently breaking off to pursue military aircraft on a training mission. A Listening Post/Observation Post manned by Federal Partner 4 reported a super-hot orb hovering at ground level that split into two objects and outpaced Call Sign 1 toward a redacted town; names, coordinates, agencies, and call signs are bracketed throughout.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This is an FBI 302 interview conducted with a senior US intelligence official regarding his first-hand account of a UAP encounter at a US military facility. USPER relayed to FBI agents that he and other federal and state personnel conducted searches to where orbs had been previously seen.  After searching the area with a helicopter, they found a “super-hot” orb hovering over the ground.  The orb is reported to have travelled for 20 miles at a speed too fast for the helicopter in pursuit.  An additional “swarm” of lights were seen moving in all directions.  A total of four or five additional orbs were seen shortly thereafter for a short time, flaring up and then down.  This pattern of four or five orbs flaring up, then down continued over the next thirty minutes across the area.

pdfs.title: FBI September 2023 Sighting - Composite Sketch
pdfs.blurb: Actual site photo with FBI Lab rendered graphic overlay depicting corroborating eyewitness reports from September 2023 of an apparent ellipsoid bronze metallic object materializing out of a bright light in the sky, 130-195 feet in length, and disappearing instantaneously.

pdfs.title: FBI September 2023 Sighting - Serial 3
pdfs.blurb: This is an FBI 302 interview conducted with a US citizen regarding their first-hand account of a UAP encounter at a US test site.  USPER described an object "metallic bronze in color."

pdfs.title: FBI September 2023 Sighting - Serial 4
pdfs.blurb: FBI FD-302 interview record (Serial 4, drafted October 2023) memorializing a September 2023 in-person interview by an FBI Special Agent of an unnamed witness who had served at a U.S. installation for fifteen years and held authority to restrict airspace for testing. Names, locations, dates and identifiers are redacted throughout. The witness recounts that around 7:15-7:30 a.m. on the date of incident, after a malfunctioning fob-controlled gate finally opened, she and her front-seat passenger observed a silent cigar-shaped object roughly the length of two or three Blackhawk helicopters, hovering and slowly drifting east-to-west southwest of their position with an intense diamond-white light ringed at its eastern end; the object disappeared after five to ten seconds, was also seen by a contractor in the second vehicle, and was unlike any aircraft or drone she had encountered in her career.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This is an FBI 302 interview conducted with a US citizen regarding their first-hand account of a UAP encounter at a US test site. USPER described an object "metallic/gray in color."

pdfs.title: FBI September 2023 Sighting - Serial 5
pdfs.blurb: FBI FD-302 interview report drafted in October 2023 documenting an FBI Special Agent's Facetime video interview of a contractor drone pilot conducted on a date in September 2023. The witness, with ten to fifteen drone-pilot hours, was on site with three other contractors for LiDAR tests when, at a gate during clear daylight conditions, he observed a linear, metallic-gray object roughly one to two Blackhawks in length with an extremely bright white light on its east side, no wings or exhaust, at approximately 5,000 feet AGL moving east-to-west parallel to the ground for five to ten seconds before the light extinguished and the object vanished. A second-vehicle passenger could not see it; the witness later reported weird dreams and trouble sleeping. Names, dates, file numbers and locations are heavily redacted on the FD-302/FD-302a forms.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This is an FBI 302 interview conducted with a US citizen regarding their first-hand account of a UAP encounter at a US test site. USPER described a "bright light over the horizon."

pdfs.title: Western US Event
pdfs.blurb: AARO briefing slide deck (four slides) characterizing four anomalous incidents witnessed by federal law enforcement special agents (designated USPER1 through USPER7) at undisclosed Western U.S. locations. 'Orbs Launching Orbs' describes three two-agent teams independently observing, at dusk over two days, transient orange 'mother' orbs that each appeared for one to two seconds and emitted groups of two to four red orbs, repeated at least five times. 'Large, Fiery Orb' covers a roughly one-minute dusk sighting by USPER5 and USPER6 of a glowing orange object 12-18 meters in diameter (per AARO measurements) hovering near a rock pinnacle. 'Dark Kite' and 'Transparent Kite' detail pre-dawn pursuits of a low silent triangular object showing red and white lights that moved laterally with zero apparent resistance, viewed via NVGs, with a follow-on sighting 30 minutes later of a similar canted, semi-transparent kite-shaped object that appeared to block a spotlight beam at about 50 yards.
pdfs.officialBlurb: This document is a summary of statements by seven US PERSONs employed by the federal government who separately reported observing several unidentified anomalous phenomena in the western United States over the course of two days in 2023.  The summary notes the US PERSONS reported four distinct categories of experiences, including observing “orbs launching other orbs” at a distance, observing a large stationary glowing orb at close estimated range, pursuing a large phenomenon near the ground, and observing a large, seemingly transparent phenomenon, reported to being akin to a “translucent kite.” Although there is no technical data directly associated with this report, contextual factors — such as these events sharing features with others reported to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO), the reporters’ credibility, and the potentially anomalous nature of the events themselves — combine to make this report among the most compelling within AARO’s current holdings.

images.title: FBI Photo A1
images.blurb:
The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of a still image derived from a U.S. government system. The date and location of the event have not been provided.  The original imagery was altered with redactions before being submitted to AARO. An accompanying mission report was not provided. The operator reported that they were unable to positively identify the UAP.

Narrative Description: The monochrome image displays a uniform, grainy texture with a central crosshair reticle. A small, dark, and slightly irregular object is visible just below and to the right of the center of the reticle. This narrative description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

images.title: NASA-UAP-VM1, Apollo 12, 1969
images.blurb:
This archival photograph depicts the lunar surface as viewed from the landing site of Apollo 12. This image features a highlighted area of interest slightly to the right of the vertical axis of the frame, above the horizon, in which unidentified phenomena are visible. 

This image has been modified from its original state to assist viewers in identifying specific areas of interest. These highlights are provided for contextual purposes only. Such alterations do not constitute an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the nature or significance of the subject matter.

images.title: NASA-UAP-VM6, Apollo 17, 1972
images.blurb: As part of the review of historical UAP materials under PURSUE, DOW has opened a case to investigate the accompanying NASA photograph from the Apollo 17 mission, taken December 1972. The image contains three “dots” in a triangular formation in the lower right quadrant of the lunar sky that is clearly visible upon magnification of the image. While this photo has been previously released and discussed by keen observers, there is no consensus about the nature of the anomaly. New preliminary US government analysis suggests the image feature is potentially the result of a physical object in the scene. Additionally, as part of this investigation, the government has obtained the original film from the Apollo 17 mission and the results of the full NASA and DOW analysis will be released when completed.

images.title: Page 114 — 342 Hs1 416511228 Box186 319.1 Flying Discs 1949
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 114 of 342_HS1-416511228_319.1 Flying Discs 1949 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: Department of the Air Force compilation of 1949 Flight Service Center reports (file 319.1) on unidentified flying objects, transmitted on Form HqMAAMA No. 7 to the Commanding General, Air Materiel Command, Wright-Patterson AFB. Reports cover sightings near Griffiss AFB, Rome, NY (signed by Lt. Col. Joseph L. Eil at Olmsted FSC); over Seattle and Medford, Oregon (Lt. Col. Earl J. Livesay, McChord FSC) including a 22 August 1949 disc observed by National Guard 143rd AC&W controllers; Greenville, SC (Major George G. Neffinger, Maxwell FSC, attributed to a weather balloon); Spokane (civilian pilot Bill Miller's eight dish-shaped objects); and Columbus, Ohio (CAA controllers Andees, Markwith, and Lutz). Mostly marked Restricted or Confidential.

images.title: Page 186 — 38 143685 Box7 Incident Summaries 1 100
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 186 of 38_143685_box7_Incident_Summaries_1-100 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: Declassified Department of War (Air Force) intelligence file from Box 7 (NND 917033) containing standardized CHECK-LIST UNIDENTIFIED FLYING OBJECTS incident summaries collected during the summer of 1947 and into early 1948, originally classified Confidential/Restricted. Each entry records observer name, occupation, location, time, altitude, speed, shape, color and reliability evaluation for sightings reported at sites such as Muroc Army Air Field, California (Lt. Joseph C. McHenry, Jannette Marie Scott, S/Sgt Joseph Ruvolo, Maj. Richard R. Shoop, Maj. J.C. Wise) and Rogers Dry Lake. Later pages cover the January 7, 1948 Godman Field, Kentucky event involving Col. Guy Hix and Capt. Thomas F. Mantell's fatal pursuit of an object in NG 869.

images.title: Page 001 — 38 143685 Box7 Incident Summaries 101 172
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 1 of 38_143685_box_Incident_Summaries_101-172 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: Declassified Air Force intelligence compilation of unidentified flying object check-list incident reports numbered 101 through 172, covering sightings between February and roughly mid-1948 across the United States. Each entry follows a standardized RESTRICTED/CONFIDENTIAL form recording date, time, location, observer, object characteristics, and remarks, with supporting witness statements, correspondence (including speculative letters from Pueblo's Norman G. Markham and analysis by Dr. Lincoln LaPaz of the Institute of Meteoritics), and Fourth Air Force investigative memoranda signed by Lt. Col. Donald L. Springer. Notable incidents include the Norcatur, Kansas fireball of 18 February 1948, B-29 crew sightings near Green River Utah, the Bakersfield California flaming-object reports, Smyrna AAF observations, and the Baltimore foreign-aircraft sound report relayed via SAC Andrews.

images.title: Page 017 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 1
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 17 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_1 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 (Section 1, 185 pages, FOIPA #993087/#1311404), comprising the Bureau's earliest flying-disc correspondence and clippings from July-August 1947. Includes newspaper articles about Reverend Joseph Brasky's circular saw disc in Grafton, Wisconsin, public letters addressed to Director J. Edgar Hoover, an August 5, 1947 memo from Hoover to Colonel L.R. Forney of War Department Intelligence forwarding citizen Fred R. Reibold's report, internal teletypes between SAC offices (Milwaukee, New Orleans, Washington), and a Western Union telegram from inventor Woodson C. Cope. Declassified under FBI Automatic Declassification Guide of May 24, 2007.

Edgar Hoover, an August 5, 1947 memo from Hoover to Colonel L.R. Forney of War Department Intelligence forwarding citizen Fred R. Reibold's report, internal teletypes between SAC offices (Milwaukee, New Orleans, Washington), and a Western Union telegram from inventor Woodson C. Cope. Declassified under FBI Automatic Declassification Guide of May 24, 2007.

images.title: Page 016 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 10
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 16 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_10 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 10 (serials 448 onward), a 184-page compilation of correspondence and internal memoranda spanning roughly 1966 through 1974 in which the Bureau handled UFO inquiries from private citizens. Letters signed by Director J. Edgar Hoover and later Acting/Director Clarence M. Kelley repeatedly tell correspondents (Mrs. Levi J. Dow of Goffstown NH, Mary Beth Martine, Ron Fraide of La Habra CA, the Ajax Ontario UFO Astronomer Club, Larry W. Bryant, J. A. Hennessey of London, and others) that investigation of unidentified flying objects falls outside FBI jurisdiction. The file also embeds AFSCA Flying Saucers International magazine clippings, a 1972 anonymous letter threatening to leak Pentagon UFO documents to the New York Times, a 1971 ACSI Army intelligence routing slip from LTC Alfred G. Keggins, and a March 1973 San Antonio FBI LHM by SA Richard T. Jesinger about Sgt. Paul Stigliano's report on a man calling himself Air Force Captain Dave T. Ozanne who solicited Waco Tribune UFO information while carrying a Top Secret folder.

images.title: Page 003 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 2
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 3 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_2 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 2 (serials 53-100), a 194-page run of 1947 cables, SAC letters, teletypes and Office Memoranda routing the earliest 'flying disc' reports to Director Hoover and Assistant Director D. M. Ladd. SAC San Francisco forwards Fourth Air Force A-2 reports from Lt. Col. Donald Springer and Maj. William R. Graham at Hamilton Field; SAC Newark documents an August 3, 1947 sighting at Hackensack NJ by Charles Casella Jr., Pvt. William Truex of Fort Dix and Joyce McFarland; SAC Detroit teletypes the Raymond Edward and Laura Lane 'fused sand' incident at Dow Chemical, Midland MI; and SAC Chicago is directed to interview Richard F. Shaver of Lily Lake IL after an anonymous July 5 telegram to AAF HQ tied him to flying-disc origins, alongside Maj. John D. Schindler Jr.'s July 7 report on saucers tracked over southern Wisconsin.

images.title: Page 022 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 3
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 22 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_3 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 3 (Serials 101-130), is a 190-page compilation of declassified records from 1947 covering the earliest 'flying disc' reports. The bundle includes Office Memorandums between SACs (San Francisco, Seattle, Butte) and FBI Director, plus correspondence and Summary of Information forms from Lt. Col. Donald L. Springer (A-2, Fourth Air Force, Hamilton Field), Major Jack C. White, and other Army Air Forces intelligence officers. Items document witness statements such as F.M. Johnson of Portland, Birmingham photograph reports, the Maury Island/Tacoma Fred Crisman and Harold Dahl affair (including Kenneth Arnold's interview and the death of Captain Davidson in the B-25 1316 crash), and sightings over Guam. The OCR-extracted text retains classification stamps ('CONFIDENTIAL,' 'RESTRICTED'), with serial numbers like 62-83894-101 through 130 visible alongside indexing marks.

images.title: Page 055 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 4
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 55 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_4 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 4 (Serials 131-185), containing 1947-1948 SAC field reports and Director Hoover correspondence on flying disc sightings. Includes a detailed Portland SAC report on the September 11, 1947 sightings by Chief of Police Leon Jenkins and Officers Adair, Caldwell, Raney, Shaylor and Cowling; an Anchorage SAC report on a July 1947 Bethel, Alaska airborne sighting by pilots Peck and Vince Daly in a DC-3; an Albany SAC report closing out informant A. Courtney Parker of South Royalton, Vermont; and Hoover acknowledgment letters to civilian correspondents including Mrs. McCaul, Mrs. Durand, Joseph Omiline, and a Port Allegany report quoting Red Cross director Joe Lippincott's claim that saucers originated in Spain.

images.title: Page 008 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 5
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 8 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_5 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 5 (Serials 186-245), 209 pages of correspondence from mid-1949 routed through Director Hoover, D.M. Ladd, Tolson and other Bureau officials. Centers on the Peter Camerlon Jones case: a letter Jones wrote to columnist Walter Winchell describing a 1947 mountain encounter near Los Angeles with a silver top-shaped object, prompting urgent teletypes to SAC Los Angeles R.B. Hood for background and interview, Ernest Cuneo of New York acting as Winchell's intermediary, and a July 26, 1949 SAC San Antonio memo on a Young Men's Business Club flying-saucer convention in Alexandria, Louisiana. Routine Bureau memoranda on flying disk and saucer reports.

images.title: Page 009 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 6
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 9 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_6 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894 Section 6 (Serials 246-301), a 271-page compilation of inter-office memoranda from 1950 covering aerial phenomena investigations. Includes A.H. Belmont's August 1950 summary memo to D.M. Ladd on green fireballs, discs and meteors near sensitive New Mexico installations, Dr. Lincoln La Paz's analysis through the University of New Mexico's Institute of Meteoritics, the Air Force/Land-Air Inc. Project Twinkle observation effort at Vaughn, NM, a September 1950 letter from FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover to USAF Director of Special Investigations forwarding a Toronto correspondent's saucer letter, and a Major General C.P. Cabell USAF directive AFOIO-CC-1 dated 8 September 1950 standardizing reporting of unconventional aircraft to Air Materiel Command. Also documents a Philadelphia Police flying-disc residue sighting on 9/26/1950.

images.title: Page 006 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 7
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 6 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_7 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 7 (Serials 302-343), comprising correspondence from August through September 1952 between Director J. Edgar Hoover, FBI field offices, and the Air Force Office of Special Investigations regarding flying saucer reports. Items include a Savannah teletype on a blue saucer-shaped light over the AEC's Savannah River Plant, Hoover form-letter replies forwarding civilian sightings (Edmond Kane, Mrs. Tygrett, Mrs. Haufe, William Hoffmeyer, John Lang, Edmond Elwell) to the Air Force, a German-language anonymous letter translated by the Cryptanalysis-Translation Section, lab examination of scoutmaster D.S. Desvergers's burned cap from West Palm Beach, and Branigan-to-Belmont memos reissuing SAC Letter #38 instructions to refer disc complaints to OSI.

Photograph page render extracted from page 201 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_7 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 7 (Serials 302-343), comprising correspondence from August through September 1952 between Director J. Edgar Hoover, FBI field offices, and the Air Force Office of Special Investigations regarding flying saucer reports. Items include a Savannah teletype on a blue saucer-shaped light over the AEC's Savannah River Plant, Hoover form-letter replies forwarding civilian sightings (Edmond Kane, Mrs. Tygrett, Mrs. Haufe, William Hoffmeyer, John Lang, Edmond Elwell) to the Air Force, a German-language anonymous letter translated by the Cryptanalysis-Translation Section, lab examination of scoutmaster D.S. Desvergers's burned cap from West Palm Beach, and Branigan-to-Belmont memos reissuing SAC Letter #38 instructions to refer disc complaints to OSI.

images.title: Page 052 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 8
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 52 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_8 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file 62-HQ-83894, Section 8 (serials 344-384), a compilation of 1954-1957 internal memoranda, airtels, and Director Hoover correspondence on flying-saucer matters. Cincinnati SAC reports on Truman Bethurum, George Hunt Williamson, and promoter Henry Maday's Taft Auditorium lecture; WFO documents Navy Bureau of Aeronautics officer John Hutson's interviews with Frances Swan of South Berwick, Maine, who claimed thought-transmission contact with extraterrestrials 'AFFA' and 'PONNAR' aboard ships M-4 and L-11, joined by retired Adm. Herbert B. Knowles and Canadian physicist Wilbur B. Smith. Additional items include a Tucson form letter from London 'Aerial Phenomena Investigator' Mattan, the Detroit Flying Saucer Club inquiry into member Randall Cox, and Wladyslaw Krasuski's 1944 sighting near Gut Alt Golssen, Germany, forwarded to NSC's J. Patrick Coyne. Lightly redacted with FOIPA stamps.

images.title: Page 100 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Section 9
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 100 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Section_9 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI headquarters file Section 9 (serials 385-447) compiling internal Bureau correspondence on flying-disc reports from late 1957 through the early 1960s, including Office Memoranda from SACs in Dallas, St. Louis, and Kansas City, a Roach-to-Belmont memo on the post-Sputnik wave of UFO sightings, and outbound letters signed by Director J. Edgar Hoover to the Air Force Director of Special Investigations referring civilian inquiries. Materials cover Robert T. Stone's Inter-Continental Aerial Research Foundation, Donald E. Keyhoe's NICAP queries, William A. Rhodes' 1947 Phoenix saucer photographs, the Aerial Phenomena Research Group, and a Sanders memo summarizing standing Bureau policy that all UFO reports be referred to the Air Force without FBI investigation.

images.title: Page 071 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Serial 130
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 71 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_130 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: Serial 130 of FBI file 62-HQ-83894, declassified under NND 90986, gathers Air Defense Command and Fourth Air Force intelligence summaries together with FBI office memoranda from 1947. Featured items include 15-18 September 1947 Headquarters Air Defense Command (Mitchel Field, NY) Summary of Information interviews with Pan American Airways Captain Alpheus O. Powell and navigator Walter I. White concerning a 4 August 1947 sighting of an orange cylindrical object near Boston during a Gander-La Guardia flight, and an 8 July 1947 Birmingham AAF report by Major Jack C. White transmitting a photograph by Age-Herald copy reader Robert Crossland of discs over Birmingham, Alabama. Additional pages reproduce newspaper clippings, witness statements from Twin Falls and Filer (Idaho) including County Commissioner Hawkins, and a Williams Field/Brooks Field interview with Lt. Eric B. Armstrong about objects near Lake Mead. Pages bear 'CONFIDENTIAL' and 'RESTRICTED' stamps and routing marks.

images.title: Page 010 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Serial 220
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 10 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_220 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI HQ Serial 220 (62-HQ-83894-EBF 220), a 15-page enclosure-behind-file consisting of a March 19, 1950 letter and inventor's manuscript from Miguel Angel Garcia Macias of Veracruz, Mexico, addressed to the President of the Commission of Scientific Investigation of the United States of North America in New York. Garcia Macias, identifying himself as an Ideographic Inventor, describes his theory of stratospheric aerostats (flying saucers) using atomic propulsion and global-conic stability, complains his prior inventions (Gote-Graduns dropper, automatic shovels) were patented by others, and encloses a Spanish-language original, hand-drawn diagrams of propellers and stability cones, and a March 16, 1950 Mexican newspaper clipping about photographs of a flying saucer over Durango by student German Horacio Robles Jr., translated by Mrs. Sophia Saliba.

images.title: Page 003 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Serial 449
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 3 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_Serial_449 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI airtel from the SAC, Los Angeles to the Director dated October 3, 1966 (file 100-24442) forwarding Issue No. 24 (July 1966) of Flying Saucers International, the official journal of the Amalgamated Flying Saucer Clubs of America headquartered at 2004 North Hoover Street, Los Angeles. The Philadelphia Division had received the magazine from Mr. Jarvis H. Cooper of the IRS, who flagged a front-page article that allegedly expounded the Communist Party line. The article, attributed to 'Master Kalen-Li Retan' of the planet Korendor and relayed via short-wave by Bob Renaud, denounces the U.S. Military-Industrial complex and the Vietnam War; Los Angeles indicates no investigation contemplated. Lightly redacted under standard FBI Automatic Declassification.

images.title: Page 099 — 65 Hs1 834228961 62 Hq 83894 Sub A
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 99 of 65_HS1-834228961_62-HQ-83894_SUB_A (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: FBI Headquarters file (62-83894 Sub A) compiling newspaper clippings and wire-service stories from the late 1940s through the mid-1960s about flying saucer sightings and UFO controversies, routed via the Bureau's clipping desk to officials including Tolson, Belmont, Mohr, Rosen, and Miss Gandy. Stories cover the 1959 American Airlines DC-6 sighting by Capt. Peter Killian over Pennsylvania, Carl Jung's 1958 statements at Alamogordo, the 1957 Reinhold Schmidt Kearney, Nebraska contactee episode, the 1953 Quantico Marines incident, Capt. Ulf Christiernsson's Swedish saucer report, and disputes between Maj. Donald Keyhoe and the Air Force over Project Blue Book findings. Pages bear FBI routing stamps and the case-file serial 62-83894-A; the file is fully unredacted under FBI's automatic declassification.

images.title: Page 001 — Dow Uap D33 Mission Report Greece October 2023
images.blurb:
Photograph embedded image extracted from page 1 of DOW-UAP-D33, Mission Report, Greece, October 2023 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 9329374) from the 33rd Special Operations Squadron documenting a 13-hour 30-minute ISR sortie launched from LGLR (Larissa, Greece) on 26-27 October 2023 and recovered at OJMS (Amman, Jordan), declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison on 22 January 2026. The aircraft, equipped with AN/DAS-4 targeting pod, AWGMESH, and Link 16, conducted FMV/SIGINT collection in support of a masked tasking under the Violent Extremist Group global campaign plan. At 0035Z on 27 October the crew observed a single circular UAP flying just above ocean water, executing sharp 90-degree turns at an estimated 80 mph before being lost from the feed at 0038Z; assessed benign with unknown propulsion. Names and tasking specifics redacted.

images.title: Page 001 — Dow Uap D35 Mission Report Greece October 2023
images.blurb:
Photograph embedded image extracted from page 1 of DOW-UAP-D35, Mission Report, Greece, October 2023 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP 9337873) from the 33rd Special Operations Squadron, 27th SOW, declassified by USCENTCOM Chief of Staff MG Richard A. Harrison on January 22, 2026 and approved for release to AARO. It covers an AFSOC AREC/ISR sortie that took off from LGLR (Larissa, Greece) at 1504Z on 28 October 2023 with FMV/SIGINT collection for a masked tasking, AGM-114R Hellfires aboard, AN/DAS-4 targeting pod and Link 16. At 0811Z on 29 October the crew observed a seemingly circular UAP flying just above the ocean surface toward land near grid 35S MV 3, lost contact moments later, and assessed it as benign with unknown propulsion. Moderately redacted with 1.4a coordinate masking and (b)(3)/(b)(6) name redactions.

At 0811Z on 29 October the crew observed a seemingly circular UAP flying just above the ocean surface toward land near grid 35S MV 3, lost contact moments later, and assessed it as benign with unknown propulsion. images.title: Page 001 — Dow Uap D38 Range Fouler Debrief Middle East May 2020
images.blurb:
Photograph embedded image extracted from page 1 of DOW-UAP-D38, Range Fouler Debrief, Middle East, May 2020 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: Range Fouler Debrief Form submitted to SPEAR documenting an ISR aircrew observation on 14 May 2020 at 20:40Z (night) at coordinates 28:31:4N 49:52:4E, altitude 20,000 feet, in the Persian Gulf. While performing an ISR tasking using ULTN/Black Hot/Lin sensors, the crew tracked a single solid white round object flying through the field of view; the object was briefly lost and reacquired, appeared to make erratic movements just above the water, and was followed at 4x zoom until track placement was lost. Radar showed an intermittent trackfile with no EA indications, AIM-9x self-track, ATFLIR autotrack or tally. Aircrew names, squadron, and contact details redacted under (b)(6); declassified by MG Richard A. Harrison.

images.title: Page 005 — Dow Uap D5 Mission Report Arabian Gulf 2020
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 5 of DOW-UAP-D5, Mission Report, Arabian Gulf, 2020 (Department of War).

FROM PARENT DOC: Heavily redacted USCENTCOM mission report fragment (six pages, with four pages reduced to a single 1.4(a) marking) preserving only two GENTEXT/UAP observation entries classified S//REL. The first records that at 1354Z the aircraft observed one UAP in the vicinity of MGRS grid 34SCE7566990098 traveling at 40 knots between FL160 and FL170 with constant velocity. The second documents an observation at 2243Z of two possible UAPs near 35TQK1580995057 estimated at 278 knots, which then increased speed and changed direction toward the south. Aircraft callsign, platform, and personnel identifiers are withheld.

images.title: Page 001 — Fbi Photo B1
images.blurb:
Photograph page render extracted from page 1 of FBI Photo B1 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of a still image derived from a U.S. military system in 2025. The original imagery was altered with redactions before being submitted to AARO. An accompanying mission report was not provided. The operator reported that they were unable to positively identify the UAP. The date in the image is incorrect due to system date/time not being set. 

Narrative Description: The monochrome image displays a grainy texture with a central crosshair reticle. A small, dark, circular object is visible in the upper right quadrant, near the center of the frame. The background appears to show an indistinct mountain range or cloud formation.This narrative description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

Photograph page render extracted from page 1 of FBI Photo B8 (FBI).

FROM PARENT DOC: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of a still image derived from a U.S. military system in 2025. The original imagery was altered with redactions before being submitted to AARO. An accompanying mission report was not provided. The operator reported that they were unable to positively identify the UAP. The date in the image is incorrect due to system date/time not being set.

Narrative Description: The monochrome image displays a grainy texture with a central crosshair reticle. A small, dark, circular object is visible just right of the center of the reticle in the top right quadrant. The background shows an indistinct mountain range. This narrative description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.title: NASA-UAP-D3A, Gemini 7 Audio Excerpt, 1965
videos.blurb: This audio recording contains air to ground communications and the NASA Public Affairs audio feed with commentary, recorded during the flight of the Gemini 7 mission. In this excerpted segment of audio, Astronaut Frank Borman reports to NASA mission control in Houston his sighting of an unidentified object, which he referred to as a "bogey." This sighting occurred on December 5, 1965. The dialogue includes Borman's initial report, as well as additional comments by Astronaut Jim Lovell, Borman's fellow crew member.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR19, Unresolved UAP Report, Middle East, May 2022
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of five seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2022. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D10, described the observation as a “possible missile” moving across the field-of-view. The report also described four other objects not depicted in the video as “possible birds.”

Video Description: At the two second mark, the video depicts an area of contrast moving from left to right across the bottom third of the sensor field-of-view.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR26, Unresolved UAP Report, United Arab Emirates, October 2023
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of 43 seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2023. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D23, mentions a UAP was observed during the mission.

Video Description:
00:00-00:17: An area of contrast remains generally within the top left quarter of the display.
00:17-00:18: The sensor pans from right to left, causing the area of contrast to pass through the center of the display. The sensor then pans from left to right, causing the area of contrast to return to its approximate initial position within the sensor field-of-view.
00:29: The sensor stops tracking the area of contrast, causing it to leave the sensor field-of-view on the left side of the screen.
00:30-00:43: The sensor resumes its motion relative to the background but does not reacquire the area of contrast.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of four minutes and 57 seconds of video footage from an infrared (IR) sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2023. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D23, mentions a UAP was observed during the mission.

Video Description:
00:00-01:55: No content.
01:56: An area of contrast becomes distinguishable against the background in the center of the right side of the display.
02:04: The IR sensor pans to center on the area of contrast.
02:14: The sensor field-of-view narrows to zoom in on the area of contrast.
02:15-03:26: The area of contrast remains generally in the center of the sensor field-of-view. 
03:27-04:57: The sensor motion causes the area of contrast to move erratically across the display. Due to this motion, the sensor system repeatedly loses and reacquires the area of contrast within the center area of the display.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR28, Unresolved UAP Report, Greece, January 2024
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of one minute and five seconds of video footage captured via multiple sensor modalities aboard a U.S. military platform in 2024. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D7, described the UAP as “diamond-shaped” and moving at approximately 434 knots. The observer also reported that the UAP was only detectable via short-wave infrared (SWIR) sensor.

Video Description: The screen is split into two viewing areas for the first ten seconds of the video, with the right side displaying electro-optical footage and the left side displaying SWIR footage.
00:04: An area of contrast becomes distinguishable against the background in the center of the right frame.
00:10: The display shifts to a full-screen view of the SWIR feed to better focus on the area of contrast.
00:55: The area of contrast remains generally within the center of the sensor field-of-view. The area of contrast visually resembles an inverted teardrop with a vertically linear trailing mass suspended below.
00:56: The operator switches the sensor modality to visible spectrum, losing the subject against the background.
00:57-01:05: The operator switches the sensor modality to SWIR (Black-Hot) but does not reacquire the area of contrast.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR34, Unresolved UAP Report, Greece, October 2023
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of two minutes and 57 seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2023. An accompanying mission report, DoW-UAP-D33, described the UAP as flying near the surface of the ocean and making multiple “90-degree turns” at approximately 80 miles per hour.

Video Description:
00:04: An area of contrast enters the sensor field-of-view from the bottom left quarter of the screen.
00:07-00:19: The area of contrast moves back and forth horizontally across the field-of-view as the sensor pans to track it.
00:20-01:00: The area of contrast remains generally centered within the sensor field-of-view.
01:00-02:01: The sensor designates the area of contrast with a blue reticle, synchronizing its motion with the area of contrast’s relative position.
02:02-02:21: The sensor engages a contrast filter to better differentiate the area of contrast from the background.
02:22: The area of contrast becomes indistinguishable against the background, and the reticle drops its lock.
02:27-02:57: After losing lock, the sensor rapidly cycles zoom levels and contrast thresholds.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

00:06-00:18: The sensor pans away from the scene’s initial subject matter while cycling contrast settings and zoom levels.
00:19: The area of contrast re-enters the sensor field-of-view from near the center of the top edge of the screen.
00:20-01:15: The area of contrast remains generally within the sensor field-of-view.
01:16: The sensor narrows its field-of-view to zoom in on the area of contrast.
01:56: The sensor further narrows its field-of-view to zoom in on the area of contrast.
02:10: A blue reticle briefly appears on screen but does not acquire a lock on the area of contrast.
02:15-02:17: The sensor switches to a different modality and loses track of the area of contrast.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR40, Unresolved UAP Report, Middle East, 2020
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of one minute and three seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2020. The original reporter digitally altered the imagery by pausing the video playback and adding a white line encircling an area of interest at timestamp 00:10, annotated with the phrase “U/I SMALL THERMAL SIGNATURE.” AARO did not edit the originally reported material, and this media is presented as received.

Video Description:
00:00-00:09: An area of contrast brightens within the sensor field-of-view, becoming increasingly distinct against the background.
00:10-00:14: Playback pauses to display a white line encircling an area of interest, annotated with the phrase “U/I SMALL THERMAL SIGNATURE.”
00:15-01:03: Playback resumes, with the sensor panning to track the area of contrast against the background, generally maintaining the area of contrast’s position within the top third of the display area. During this period, the sensor cycles through several contrast and zoom settings.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of four minutes and 53 seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2020. The reporter did not provide any oral or written description of the observation.

Video Description:
00:00-00:12: An area of contrast enters the sensor field-of-view from the bottom left corner of the frame. The sensor pans to track the area of contrast, keeping it generally within the lower left quadrant of the frame.
00:13-00:40: The area of contrast intermittently loses distinctiveness against the background, seeming to disappear and reappear irregularly.
00:41: The sensor narrows its field-of-view to zoom in on the area of contrast.
00:42-00:52: An area of contrast exits the sensor field-of-view from the left half of the top of the frame, after which the sensor pans up and to the left.
00:53-02:09: The sensor pans erratically, with an area of contrast appearing in a relatively fixed position to the left and slightly below the center of the frame.
02:09-02:29: The sensor switches imaging modalities, and the area of contrast appears to the right and slightly above the center of the frame.
02:30-04:53: The sensor switches imaging modalities again, and the area of contrast returns to its previous position to the left and slightly below the center of the frame.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR44, Unresolved UAP Report, Middle East, 2020
videos.blurb:
The United States Central Command submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP) to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of five minutes and 11 seconds of video footage from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2020. The reporter did not provide any oral or written description of the observation.

Video Description: This video features incidentally recorded audio, which does not relate to the visual content described below.
00:00-00:30: No content.
00:31-03:24: The sensor pans down and to the right to focus on an area of contrast. The sensor tracks the area of contrast against the background for approximately three minutes, panning to maintain its position generally within the center of the frame. The sensor cycles contrast and zoom levels several times throughout, appearing as brief, bright white flashes across the frame.
03:25-04:23: The sensor cycles through reticles of various sizes while continuing to track the area of contrast. Between 04:20 and 04:23, the area of contrast briefly leaves the center of the sensor field-of-view.
04:24-04:50: The sensor field-of-view widens to zoom out from the scene, continuing to track the area of contrast.
04:50-04:54: The sensor stops tracking the area of contrast, at which point it exits the frame from the top left quadrant of the screen.
04:55-05:11: No content.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.

00:04-00:30: The area of contrast gradually increases in distinctiveness against the background.
00:31: The sensor narrows its field-of-view to zoom in on the area of contrast.
00:32-00:56: The area of contrast increases in apparent size and distinctiveness.
00:57-00:58: The area of contrast leaves the center of the frame and passes out of the sensor field-of-view, exiting the scene in the bottom right corner of the screen.

AARO Comment: The area of contrast’s apparent increase in size is likely to be at least partially attributable to the U.S. platform closing the distance between itself and the source of the detection.

videos.title: DOW-UAP-PR49, Unresolved UAP Report, Department of the Army, 2026
videos.blurb:
The Department of the Army submitted a report of an unidentified anomalous phenomenon to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) consisting of one minute and 49 seconds of video from an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2026. The reporter did not provide any oral or written description of the observation.

Video Description:
00:00-00:08: The sensor tracks an initial area of interest.
00:09-00:16: The sensor disengages from its previous area of focus and pans from right to left to track two areas of contrast, narrowing the field-of-view to zoom in while panning to maintain the objects' positions generally within the center of the frame.
00:17-01:03: The sensor widens its field-of-view to zoom out, keeping the areas of contrast generally centered within the display.
01:04-01:08: The sensor field-of-view rapidly cycles between levels of zoom, causing the areas of contrast to appear to rapidly increase and decrease in size.
01:09-01:48: The sensor tracks the areas of contrast while maintaining a generally centered position, intermittently cycling between contrast settings.

This video description is provided for informational purposes only. Readers should not interpret any part of this description as reflecting an analytical judgment, investigative conclusion, or factual determination regarding the described event’s validity, nature, or significance.
